- Why Are Miami Yacht Charters So Limited In Peak Season? - Everything About Miami: Kind: captions Language: en Why are Miami yacht charters so limited in peak season? Imagine cruising through Miami's sparkling waters on a luxurious yacht, feeling the warm sun and cool breeze. Now, picture trying to book that yacht during the busiest time of the year and finding it's almost impossible. That's because Miami yacht charters are limited during peak season. Miami's peak season runs from December to February when the weather is perfect. The temperatures are warm, skies are clear, and the ocean is calm. This attracts thousands of visitors escaping colder climates all eager to enjoy water activities like sailing, fishing, and relaxing on yachts. The high demand for these experiences makes it tough to find available boats. One big reason for the limited availability is the strict regulations. The United States Coast Guard limits private charter yachts to a maximum of 13 passengers plus crew. This rule applies no matter how big the yacht is. So even if you rent a large vessel, only a small group can go at once. This naturally reduces how many people can enjoy yacht charters during busy times. Safety rules also play a role. Miami's maritime laws requiring yachts to meet specific safety standards and licensing. These regulations mean only certain vessels can operate as charters. The Passenger Vessel Safety Act enforces these rules which limits the number of boats available for rent during peak season. Another factor is the need to book early. Because demand skyrockets during the winter months, many people reserve their spots months in advance. Last minute bookings are rare, especially for weekends or special events like New Year's Eve parties. If you want to go on a yacht during peak season, planning ahead is essential. Weather can sometimes cause issues, too. Even though winter weather is usually good, storms or hurricanes can happen unexpectedly. These weather events can lead to cancellations or rescheduling, reducing the number of trips that can be completed. Yacht captains prioritize safety and may postpone trips if conditions are unsafe. Operational constraints also matter. Many yachts offer different rental lengths from short 2-hour trips to fullday adventures. Some yachts are booked for parties or corporate events, which limits available slots for other customers. The busy schedule during peak season means fewer open times for everyone. Overall, the combination of high demand, strict passenger limits, safety regulations, and weather considerations makes yacht charters during peak season quite limited in Miami. If you want to enjoy a yacht during this time, it's best to plan and book well in advance. Miami's authorities aim to keep water activities safe and enjoyable for everyone. So, respecting these rules helps keep the experience fun and secure for all. [Music]

- Boat Show Survival Guide | Avoid These 9 Rookie Mistakes: Kind: captions Language: en Whether you're a first-time visitor or a seasoned&nbsp; boat show veteran, the Miami International Boat&nbsp;&nbsp; Show can be overwhelming. With over a thousand&nbsp; boats spread across multiple venues and hundreds&nbsp;&nbsp; of exhibitors, countless distractions competing&nbsp; for your attention, it is easy to waste time and&nbsp;&nbsp; miss what matters most. I'm Maryline Bossar with&nbsp; Current Yachts, and today I am sharing our top&nbsp;&nbsp; strategies for getting the most out of the Miami&nbsp; show. These are the same tips that we give our&nbsp;&nbsp; clients who are serious about finding their next&nbsp; boat. Let's go. Tip number one, do your homework&nbsp;&nbsp; before you go. Here's the reality. If you show up&nbsp; unprepared, you'll spend half your day looking at&nbsp;&nbsp; gear and accessories for boats you'll never buy.&nbsp; So, before you set foot on the docks, research&nbsp;&nbsp; which manufacturers will be there and create a&nbsp; short list of specific models you want to see.&nbsp;&nbsp; Spend time online studying these boats, their&nbsp; specifications, layouts, and pricing. Know&nbsp;&nbsp; your must-sees versus your nice-to-sees. The&nbsp; Miami show is spread across multiple location,&nbsp;&nbsp; so understanding where specific boats will&nbsp; be displayed saves you hours of walking. And&nbsp;&nbsp; don't forget logistics. Plan your parking&nbsp; or transportation strategy ahead of time.&nbsp;&nbsp; Miami traffic during the boat show is no&nbsp; joke. Tip number two, make appointments.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is where serious buyers separate themselves&nbsp; from casual browsers. If you've identified a&nbsp;&nbsp; dealer or a specific yacht that you are interested&nbsp; in seeing, reach out before the show and schedule&nbsp;&nbsp; an appointment. This signals that you're a&nbsp; qualified buyer, and that's what you get in&nbsp;&nbsp; return. You skip the lines, you get dedicated&nbsp; time with a knowledgeable representative,&nbsp;&nbsp; and you might even hear about special show&nbsp; incentives before the show even begins. Pro tip,&nbsp;&nbsp; ask to be the first appointment of the day. Shows&nbsp; get hectic and while everyone has good intentions,&nbsp;&nbsp; appointments can run late. So being first means&nbsp; you're not the one left waiting. And here is&nbsp;&nbsp; another practical tip. Bring business cards&nbsp; with your name, email, phone, and boating area.&nbsp;&nbsp; Perhaps the boat you own already, because most&nbsp; manufacturer booths will require that information&nbsp;&nbsp; anyway. So having the card saves you time and&nbsp; prevents you from having to ride it 20 times a&nbsp;&nbsp; day. By the way, if you're looking for tickets to&nbsp; the Miami show, check out the description below.&nbsp;&nbsp; I will link to our calendar of events where you&nbsp; can make an appointment with us and request your&nbsp;&nbsp; show tickets compliments of Current Yachts. Tip&nbsp; number three, create a game plan. Before you go&nbsp;&nbsp; to the Miami show, ask yourself, are you there to&nbsp; research or to buy? If you're in research mode,&nbsp;&nbsp; that's fine. But if you're ready to make a&nbsp; decision, tell the salesperson or your broker&nbsp;&nbsp; beforehand so they can prepare offers and have&nbsp; the right people [music] available. The Miami show&nbsp;&nbsp; can have well over 100,000 visitors, so map out&nbsp; your day based on priorities, which venues need&nbsp;&nbsp; to be hit first, which boats are non negotiable.&nbsp; Because of traffic, we talked about that earlier,&nbsp;&nbsp; we recommend you choose and stick to one venue&nbsp; per day. So that will limit the back and forth&nbsp;&nbsp; between, say, Miami Beach and the convention&nbsp; center. So consider timing strategically. Arrive&nbsp;&nbsp; early in the morning or later in the afternoon to&nbsp; avoid the peak times and if you're close to making&nbsp;&nbsp; a decision, plan to arrive a few days early or&nbsp; afterwards for any trial run opportunities. This&nbsp;&nbsp; is when serious deals get done. Tip number&nbsp; four, dress appropriately. You'll be on your&nbsp;&nbsp; feet all day climbing in and out of boats.&nbsp; So wear comfortable casual clothes and bring&nbsp;&nbsp; layers for changing weather conditions. Now, I&nbsp; know this is Miami, but today is the day you'll&nbsp;&nbsp; leave your high heels at home and even your lace&nbsp; up shoes because you'll be taking your shoes off&nbsp;&nbsp; constantly when boarding. So, go barefoot or&nbsp; bring spare socks and travel light cause most&nbsp;&nbsp; boats won't allow large bags or backpacks on&nbsp; board. And keep a small bag for the essentials&nbsp;&nbsp; and your valuables on your person. Don't forget&nbsp; sunscreen, sunglasses, water bottle, and rain&nbsp;&nbsp; jacket. This is Miami after all. Tip number five,&nbsp; prepare your questions. Don't be intimidated.&nbsp;&nbsp; Exhibitors are there to help and no question is&nbsp; too basic. Come prepared with specific questions&nbsp;&nbsp; like fuel efficiency, handling characteristics in&nbsp; various sea conditions, maintenance schedules and&nbsp;&nbsp; cost, warranty coverage. If you're getting close&nbsp; to a purchase, go deeper. Meet with a technical&nbsp;&nbsp; director to discuss your build specifications,&nbsp; electrical systems, electronics packages,&nbsp;&nbsp; and any custom work you're considering. And if&nbsp; you haven't already, educate yourself on the&nbsp;&nbsp; commissioning process. It matters so much. Tip&nbsp; number six, document everything. After seeing&nbsp;&nbsp; 20 boats, they all start to blend together. Trust&nbsp; me. Bring a notepad or use your phone to record&nbsp;&nbsp; your impressions. What did you like? What concerns&nbsp; came up? What questions do you need answered? Take&nbsp;&nbsp; photos, sure, but more importantly, take notes.&nbsp; When you're back at your hotel comparing options,&nbsp;&nbsp; you'll be grateful for the details that you've&nbsp; captured. and we've created a very simple&nbsp;&nbsp; spreadsheet for you to list the boats you're&nbsp; interested in with columns for key features,&nbsp;&nbsp; pros and cons, and pricing. Follow the link&nbsp; below to download it for free. Tip number seven,&nbsp;&nbsp; connect with other attendees. Some of your best&nbsp; insights will come from conversations with other&nbsp;&nbsp; Boers. Riding a shuttle bus or water taxi, it is&nbsp; a great time to strike up conversations. People&nbsp;&nbsp; love talking about their experiences, which boats&nbsp; they've owned, which dealers they've worked with,&nbsp;&nbsp; and which cruising grounds they recommend. You&nbsp; never know, but valuable advice or connection&nbsp;&nbsp; might come from a casual dock conversation. And&nbsp; of course, boat show going is always more fun&nbsp;&nbsp; with a friend, so be sure to share this video&nbsp; with them. And if they're a friend, they might&nbsp;&nbsp; buy your boat show ticket this year. Tip number&nbsp; eight, don't rush your decision. This is the&nbsp;&nbsp; most important tip. Decide beforehand if you're&nbsp; here to buy. If this isn't your buying trip, give&nbsp;&nbsp; yourself time to reflect after the show. There's&nbsp; no need to rush, and you definitely don't want&nbsp;&nbsp; a boat show buzz to cause a hasty decision you'll&nbsp; regret tomorrow. If you are seriously considering&nbsp;&nbsp; a specific boat, however, and just need time to&nbsp; think, ask about a hull reservation or an option&nbsp;&nbsp; period, typically 7 to 14 days. It gives you time&nbsp; and breathing room to make a thoughtful decision&nbsp;&nbsp; rather than an emotional one. And so as you have&nbsp; more time, you can research more the dealership,&nbsp;&nbsp; the brand, or the new model that you may have&nbsp; stumbled upon. Tip number nine, enjoy yourself,&nbsp;&nbsp; but pace it. The Miami Show is as much a social&nbsp; event as it is a shopping experience. So take&nbsp;&nbsp; it in and enjoy yourself. But here's a fair&nbsp; warning. The show runs multiple days and there&nbsp;&nbsp; are networking events and parties and plenty of&nbsp; cocktails. If you burn out on opening night in&nbsp;&nbsp; Miami Beach, you'll miss the rest of the show. So&nbsp; pace yourself. The Miami International Boat Show&nbsp;&nbsp; is one of the world's premier yachting events,&nbsp; and with the right preparation and strategy,&nbsp;&nbsp; it can be an incredibly fun and productive&nbsp; experience. Let us know in the comments what your&nbsp;&nbsp; favorite part of the Miami boat show is, and if&nbsp; we missed any important boat show survival tips,&nbsp;&nbsp; we'll make sure to pin the best one. At Current&nbsp; Yachts, we guide buyers and sellers through every&nbsp;&nbsp; step of the yacht transaction process. If you're&nbsp; thinking about attending Miami or any other boat&nbsp;&nbsp; show and want expert guidance, get in touch with&nbsp; us. Visit currentyachts.com. We're here to help&nbsp;&nbsp; you navigate both the purchase process and the&nbsp; Miami boat show. We'll see you on the docks.

  2. @Robert-pg2id [1 likes]: I have a list of over 25+ reasons to move to and live in Connecticut. May I list a few?
.5) Half of the US Navy's submarines and 1/5 of all jet engines in the world are made in Connecticut.
1.) History: Although vehemently denied by the Smithsonian, the TRUE FIRST IN FLIGHT honor goes to Gustave Whitehead who flew over 1/2 mile in Bridgeport, Connecticut in 1901, 2 years before the Wright Brothers. There are plenty of investigative reports that bear this out, some are on YouTube, & the State of Connecticut legislated a special day honoring Gustave Whitehead for this achievement.
2.) Climate: Conn. has the stable range of all 4 seasons (without the danger you see on the news of some areas in the US) with beautiful foliage in the Autumn, try Route 7 up the Housatonic River, dotted with Covered bridges, restaurants and antique shops all the way to the Mass. border.
3.) Sports: 2 AHL Hockey Teams in Bridgeport & Hartford (newly renovated stadium), 2 Baseball Teams in Hartford and Norwich, CT. (Hartford Stadium described as best new stadium in MiLB). There are 4 ski Mountains in Conn., 4 NASCAR Racetracks including Lime Rock Speedway which is frequented by more than a few movie stars. There are over 170 Golf Courses in CT both public & private.
4.) Universities: Home of perennial NCAA National Champions UCONN & Quinnipiac, also Yale University in New Haven, CT now rated among the top 10 Universities IN THE WORLD. Connecticut also runs its own State-level High School System that will teach your children a trade of their choice such as Nursing, Plumbing, Manufacturing, CAD design & more for FREE and help them qualify for a Professional License. How's that for a good start for any 18-year-old? This state-run High School System has its own campuses and sports teams.
5.) Vineyards:  There are over 35 Vineyards in Conn., with award winning vintages. Some even have their own restaurants making you feel like you're in France!
6.) Military: CT is the home of the US Coast Guard in New London, CT and also a US Navy Submarine base in Groton, CT. BTW, you can tour a real Navy sub for free at this Naval base in Groton.
7.) More history: Capt. Kidd is noted as depositing his treasure on an island up the Connecticut River and along the Housatonic River near Stratford & Shelton, CT. Ancient history has a historical site called "Gungywamp" near Groton, CT and is said to date all the way to about 900 AD & is credited to ancient Celtic explorers.
8.) Travel: The are over 30 airports & airfields in Conn., some offering Regional and International flights.
9.) Zoos & Wildlife exhibits: There is Beardsley Zoo in Bridgeport plus 2 Maritime Museums in Norwalk & Mystic, Connecticut, with live hands on exhibits. There are also smaller ones like at Hammonasset State Park along the Connecticut Coastline. 
10.) Proximity: NYC & Boston are an easy drive from anywhere in CT (1-1/2 hrs. on average), so you can work, visit, and enjoy what they offer, but don't have to live in the middle of their overcrowding and resource starved cities.
11.) The Connecticut coast is dotted with almost 50 islands, some you can AirBnB, others have homes, lighthouses, one was the Summer White House of President Taft and another island has a US Wildlife Lab & pavilion you can access by water taxi, and the Park Rangers there will tour you the island for free. There are water-tours available of these islands called the Thimble Islands.
12.) Connecticut is home of famous stars such as Meg Ryan, Michael Bolton, Paul Newman, the Carpenters, Secretary of State Henry Kissinger and the Mayor of Mexico City both lived in Litchfield County, Conn.
13.) Agriculture #1: The 3 largest dairies in Conn. have over 2,000 cows each and are a very large supplier to famous cheeses like Cabot cheese & others. Some of these dairies you can tour for free - they use huge rotary milking platforms to do 'their work' and each herd happily co-operates!
14.) Agriculture #2: Connecticut is the nation's largest supplier of black currant and a major (very major supplier) of: lavender, apples & sunflowers.

  5. @sailingsvmutiny1762: Great breakdown. Especially good to see deck core and keel bolts getting attention — that’s where a lot of people get caught.

One thing I’ve noticed though (especially in this price range):
it’s usually not the obvious issues that hurt you… it’s what isn’t mentioned in listings or walkthroughs.

Things like:
– how old the standing rigging actually is vs “looks fine”
– partial refits that skip structural work
– systems that work… but are at end-of-life

That’s where budgets quietly double after purchase.

With so many available, we would imagine there might be some negotiation there for you to leverage against the seller. You also did some other boating mistakes. Towing anyone on an inflatable, they need to have a life jacket on (your son wasn’t wearing one). Emerald Bay does not allow the use of towables, and while you were towing the family, there was no spotter in the boat. 
These are basic safety rules. Since it was your first time at Tahoe, you should’ve familiarized yourself with what the rules are and the dangers of cold water. 
Glad you enjoyed your visit, but when boating a new lake or are unfamiliar with waters, get information on rules. Tahoe has a boating app that is very helpful.

- Mussel Ridge 54' Pretti Rose Assembly - Newport Yacht Collaborative: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] on november 17th the pre-constructed accommodations module rolled out of the shed its complex geometry on display laser scans had confirmed its fit with a hull delivered from the builder in main the module which has multiple landing surfaces fit on the first try it lay against the floors in the bilge with the bulkhead snug against the hull sides with a three millimeter tolerance in the foreground you can see the v drive transmission already installed on november 18th the pre-constructed pilot house module was ready to go it holds engine room mechanical bunk and cabin spaces structure includes two inch knife decor for the deck and one inch foam and glass for secondary structure and furniture the galley is finished painted ready for appliances the plywood building jig allowed us to do preliminary layout and fabrication of mechanical systems in this time lapse footage we lower the module into the boat with all plywood stripped away the multiple surfaces fit precisely against the structure in the hull a small amount of trimming was needed to clear the deck flange and then it landed with a perfect snug fit the overall process took less than 90 minutes with both modules in place the boat rolls into the build space to complete the interior and mechanical installations the deck and house arrived and were installed before christmas during that month we leveled the boat and the interior modules were bonded and tabbed to the hull primed and painted painting includes all bilges and mechanical spaces we began to put the hydraulic systems in place painted the bulkheads and installed the remaining interior pieces by the end of december the interior and the house were in place and the aft deck was glued down the 54-footer is now ready for completion for a spring launch

Hedge funds are secretive firms that have a wide range of investing styles and there are key part of the Wall Street South ecosystem. But they've historically only been open to institutional investors or very wealthy individuals and they usually charge high fees. While mutual funds, exchange traded funds, and banks are heavily regulated, hedge funds face fewer rules, giving them the freedom to take bigger risks, leading to spectacular gains and sometimes spectacular implosions. The term hedge fund originally came from the idea of hedging your bets, making one bet and then making another to protect yourself. If things go the other way. Think of a street vendor selling sunglasses. They're betting on sunny weather, but a smart vendor also invests a little in umbrellas too, just in case it rains. A hedge fund is a bit like that, ready to make money in any weather. But today, the term hedge fund has evolved dramatically. Now, hedge funds use a variety of strategies and bet on everything from currencies to election outcomes. And as of the end of 2024, the industry was managing more than 4.5 trillion in assets. - Total $ Cost to Buy a Boat & Run it From Maine to Florida: Kind: captions Language: en for those of you that don't know me my name is gus and i took a boat from portland maine to miami florida this is exactly how much money it took me to buy a boat and take it down the entire east coast yachting is not only reserved for the rich and famous and i'd like to be living proof that even those without a lot of money like myself can live the wonderful boat life you don't need a multi-million dollar yacht like these to have just as much fun so this video is going to be all about how much it cost me to take this boat from maine to florida [Music] wow some of you may be thinking how did this 23 year old kid afford to do this i saw everything in the comments section from he's a trust fund kid to mommy and daddy supported this trip well my parents offered me every piece of support that i could ever need but no financial support this was on me this is actually not as expensive as you may think and with some hard work it's possible for anyone the first and main expense of this entire trip was buying the boat the boat was 25 000 but rest assured you can spend a lot less than 25 000 and still get a boat capable of doing this trip the total amount of money that i had to put into this boat before we left for the trip was 11 956 dollars this number reflects material only since i was able to do all the work myself i saved a ton of money this included everything from wiring to heavy duty alternators new bilge pumps new float switches two new battery charging systems a new main battery switch new batteries a new inverter rebuilt toilet pumps all new sewage lines a new sewage tank a new fuel tank all led lighting throughout the cabins a new hot water heater a state-of-the-art garmin navigation system loaded with some of the best charts available a fresh coat of bottom paint a new oil pan on the starboard side engine a new shaft coupler on the starboard side transmission all new shaft packing for the rudders and the prop shafts and much much more so now for the question that i've heard again and again how much fuel did it really take to get this boat from maine to florida our total fuel burn on this entire trip all the way from portland maine to miami florida totaled to be 3732.86 the 1031.12 gallons at an average of 3.62 cents per gallon some of the cheapest fuel we got was in chesapeake city maryland and it was three dollars and 10 cents per gallon some of the most expensive fuel we got was in atlantic city that was four dollars and 20 cents per gallon now fuel is rising and uh it's going to make this trip a little bit more expensive our average fuel cost down the entire east coast was 3.62 cents per gallon my boat is designated as what's called a fast trawler meaning the hole is a modified v-hull type boat it is also known as a semi-planing hole this is so that you can cruise at a faster speed than a full displacement trawler well you might be wondering why would you buy a slow trawler if you could buy a fast trawler well one of the main sacrifices is that you lose your full peel a full kill helps a lot with stability so when you don't have one you lose some stability also the keel provides some prop protection for this boat that doesn't seem to be much of an issue however we decided to take the cruise at full displacement speed which immensely helped with our fuel savings my average cruise speed was eight knots at 1300 rpm with an average fuel burn of four gallons per hour some of you may remember the day that we cruised up the delaware bay to the cnd canal we had to get some miles under our belt so that we wouldn't get stuck in the delaware bay that day we ran the boat at 16 knots at 2200 rpm just to give you an idea of the difference in fuel burn at that speed we burned an average of 32 gallons per hour that day that was an expensive day but our total fuel costs for the trip reflect that day as well one of the biggest costs for people that are traveling by boat is transient dockage and transient mortgage that is one thing that we did not spend a lot of money on we mostly anchored this resulted in insane cost savings when it came to transient dockage a lot of boaters like the security of being tied up to a dock at the end of their trip and they don't have to worry about dragging their anchor they're also supplied with shore power fresh water and other amenities of being at a marina in my opinion anchoring is what really ties together an amazing boating trip we anchored in extremely remote places like sandhol creek in north carolina you go outside and there is nothing around you it is great and the best part about it it's free if you wanted to factor transient dockage into your trip expenses you'd need to consider at least two to four dollars per foot per marina that you stop at the pricing depends mostly on the location of the marina my total transient dockage and mortgage costs were 371 dollars on the entire trip down the coast the only places that we paid for mortgage and dockage were new york city it was 120 for the night we wanted to go into the city and we weren't comfortable leaving the boat on anchor so we could have the peace of mind of leaving the boat safe while we explored the city in moorhead city north carolina we paid 20 dollars to stay on the sanitary restaurant dock we did this because it just made sense in saint augustine we rented a mooring for 26 because it was rather windy and we wanted to go to shore once we got to miami we spent 205 dollars on a mooring for a week another huge expense on any boat trip is maintenance costs i made sure my boat was in tip-top shape before we left for the trip which resulted in very few issues one of the issues that i had was one of my rebuilt raw water pumps fell apart unfortunately i check my engine bay at least once a day while we're underway and i noticed this pump was leaking before it completely failed so i decided to just buy a brand new pump 450 raw water pump most of the issues that the boat had on the trip were small issues that did not require any parts to fix rather just some labor and diagnostics a lot of repairs are what i'd call labor only repairs wiring issues little leaks things that just need a hose clamp and a new terminal saltwater boats have this inherent issue with corroding parts everything turns to crap in salt water anybody can fix their own boat it's just a matter of taking the time to go on the internet and learn about fixing your own boat if you do this you will save a ton of money i did not keep track of the hours that i put into my boat labor-wise however i would estimate at the very least i put 250 hours worth of labor into my boat i would say the average marina charges about 100 to 120 dollars per hour for their time yeah do the math on that one my only other maintenance cost on this entire trip was one oil change that i did in west palm beach that oil change cost me 178 dollars if i were to have done this trip on a sailboat i could cut my fuel costs at least in half if not even more one totally avoidable expense that i'll call personal pleasure which includes going out to restaurants and bars and stuff like that was thirteen hundred and fifty six dollars that's what i spent total on restaurants and bars and also includes a new wetsuit that i bought in south - 💸 The REAL Cost of Living on a Boat in California | Marina Fees, Liveaboard Costs & Hidden Expenses: Kind: captions Language: en You want to live on a boat in California? You might want to think again. Monthly slip fees are some of the highest in the country. Brennan Island 350 a month. Brisbane 400 a month. Sacramento 500 a month. Yacht Haven Marina 600. Pier 32700. And don't even think about getting anywhere near Long Beach. King Harbor Marina is 1,200 a month. [Music] Living on a boat in California sounds like the ultimate dream. Waking up on the water, sunsets over the bay, and maybe even saving a little money compared to renting an apartment. But what does it actually cost? Today, we're going to break down the real numbers of living on a boat in California. From the hidden fees to the big expenses, and even which regions are cheaper than others. Spoiler though, the dream is still alive, but it's not as cheap as some people think. Let's start with the biggest ongoing expense, your slip fee. A slip is basically a parking spot for the boat. And in California, slip rates are usually charged by the foot per month, and the range is wide. In the Bay Area, you're looking at roughly $8 to $15 per foot per month, depending on the marina. So, if you have like, let's say, a 35 ft sailboat, that's anywhere between 300 and 550 bucks every month just to tie the boat up. But if you head south into San Diego or Los Angeles, things get a little bit spicy. There, slips can run $25 to even $50 per foot. That same 35 ft boat suddenly cost you $900 to $1,700 a month to tie up. But here's the kicker. Not every marina allows you to live on your boat, and if they do, they almost always tack on a live aboard fee. Think of it like rent for the right to actually sleep on your boat. At Berkeley Marina in the Bay Area, that's an extra $220 bucks every month for the liabboard fee. At Treasure Island Yacht Harbor, the liverboard fee is $337. In San Diego at Pier 32, it's 500 a month for a single person and nearly 600 a month for a couple. At Konakai Marina, a resort style spot on Shelter Island. They charge about $600 just for the privilege of living aboard on top of some of the highest slip fees in the state. Now, also utilities. You think you're surrounded by water and that means unlimited access, right? Not quite. Most marinas provide dockside water, but electricity is often metered and built separately. If you run heaters or dehumidifiers or live aboard full-time with a fridge and lights and maybe even a little space heater for those chilly bay nights, that can add up. Some marinas include power, but don't count on that. Then you've got sewage pumpouts, showers, laundry, trash service, parking, and even Wi-Fi. Every marina is a little bit different. Some of them will bundle these things in for you, while others will nickel and dime you for everything. Maintenance, too, is where a lot of people underestimate the cost of even living on a boat. Regardless of where you are, boats are needy. They demand constant attention, whether it's scrubbing the bottom, painting the hull, checking for corrosion, or servicing the engine. A good rule of thumb is about 10% of the boat's value per year. So, if you bought a $50,000 boat, budget about $5,000 a year to keep it safe and seaorthy, and that should cover all the routine stuff and some of the major things. But when major things happen, you can be looking at some serious bills. Living in saltwater accelerates wear, so Bay Area or coastal SoCal boats usually need more frequent bottom paint and anode replacement compared to boats in freshwater like up the Sacramento Delta. And speaking of Delta, let's talk geography. Costs vary dramatically depending on where you want to keep this boat. inland like in Sacramento or the Delta, you can score a slip for a fraction of what they cost on the coast. At Sacramento Marina, a slip for a 40 foot boat might cost you 500 bucks a month. Brennan Island in the Delta is even cheaper. A 50ft boat there might only run you 350 bucks a month. That's less than half the cost of a comparable slip in San Diego. Compare that to King Harbor in Roondo Beach. A 40ft slip there will cost you about 1,200 a month. And that's before you add on the liboard fees, the electricity, and your maintenance. Same size boat, totally different financial reality. So, let's break it down into rough monthly budgets. If you've got a smaller 25 to 35 ft sailboat in an inland delta marina, living aboard could be as low as 1,000 to 1,800 a month all in. That covers the slip, utilities, insurance, maintenance, live aboard fees, and your food. If you got a midsize, let's say 35 to 45footer in a nicer Bay Area or Southern California marina, you're realistically looking at $2 to $35,000 a month. And if you're living the dream on a 50-footer in San Diego's Konai Marina, $5,000 a month is not out of the question. And don't forget the big lumpy expenses. Every couple years, you're going to need a hollow out to inspect and repaint the bottom. That's easily $2 to $5,000 depending on what the boat needs and how big it is. Insurance is another one you have to think about. Liability insurance is required in most marinas, but full coverage depends on your vessel's value and where you're going to sail it. Then there's fuel and propane for cooking and all the little upgrades you'll want once you live aboard for a while. Solar panels, better batteries, a dinghy, maybe Starlink so you have better internet, and all of that's going to add up. And let's talk about weight lists for a second. You can't just rock up to a California marina with your boat and get a slip right now. Give them a call and usually you'll be added to a wait list. Sometimes years long. Some marinas demand the first month slip fees upfront just to be added to that list. You get them back if you actually take possession of the slip. A lot of people have been sailing their boats up to the Pacific Northwest for a slip while they sit on the California weight list, but the PNW is getting full, too. Ventura West Marina is one of the most popular for liaborts and one of the few to actually advertise it on their website and will put you on a wait list without a deposit. Supply and demand has a big part of why these fees are so high. There's just so many boats and not enough slips. But there's something else to consider, too. It's not always the marina that's gouging the boat owner. The Bay Conservation and Development Commission imposes rules and regulations on marinas, too. like one such rule which only allows 10% of slips to be given to liverboards. And like Florida, California regulations can change. Your marina's ownership can change. Local bylaws can change. So even if you find the perfect liabboard slip at a friendly marina that you can actually afford, it doesn't mean that that won't change. You could at any time end up among the thousands of boat owners looking for a new home for their boat and a new home for themselves. Now, with all that said, living on a boat in California can still make financial sense. Rent for a one-bedroom in San Francisco or San Diego can easily hit three grand a month. In comparison, a smaller boat in the Bay Area or Delta could be a lot cheaper, especially if you own the boat already. You're not just paying for housing here, too. You're paying for a lifestyle on a boat. Sunsets on the deck, the freedom to untie the lines and go sailing, and a community of other live aboards who share the same dream as you. Let's see if we can get some realtime information in the comments here. If you live on a boat in California or are trying to, please leave a comment. How much does it cost you? What advice do you have for wouldbe boat owners coming to California? And what would you do different if you were them? I can't wait to hear from you. Also, speaking of cost-effective liab, did you see the article we did on the Catalina 375? This boat is a very hot seller for some very good reasons. An easy boat to live on, to sail, and to own, but they're getting harder to find. A great read if you're up for some learning today. I'll leave a link in the description. If you like sailboat videos, please give this one a thumbs up and hit subscribe so that I can see you again next time.

  11. @raydaugherty3046: I lived aboard in Alameda and then Richmond for 5.5 years between 2015 and 2020. Moved on land for several years and now live aboard again. During the time I was landlocked, many of the marinas were purchased by a conglomerate. And, I can still work on the boat myself at the yards here. Plan is to get all done I want done, and then do the transient thing in the Bay. That will be more expensive than where I berth, but the life there is worth it to me. 

When I lived aboard previously, I never had an issue with the waiting lists. I always presented myself in person to the Harbor Master and had current pics of my boat. I had more opportunities than I could take. Idk with the current ownership if that is still a viable plan, hence the transient thing - which is actually fine with me. It's not easy here, but it still can be done if you work at it, just like most things.
  12. @TaihamXMetroRat: 0:35 nothing about living in California sounds like a dream. The only dream with California is the dreaming being done by whoever thinks it's a good idea to set foot in that shithole 😂😂😂
  13. @robertfreeland5449: $15/month? Those are rookie numbers! Here on Tampa Bay, the municipal marinas might be in that range, but they're all full, with multi-year waiting lists. There are slips available in private marinas, but they cost a lot more. There's a nice (but not "resort-level") marina up the Manatee River near the mouth of Tampa Bay called the Twin Dolphin marina. Even for boaters making an annual commitment, their slip fee is $28/foot/month. If you're a liveaboard, they tack on another $370/month for the first liveaboard, and $250/month for each successive person. Then 50-amp electrical hookup is another $100/month... or $175/month for a liveaboard. There's even a fee for paying with a credit card vs. cash! On the plus side, you get showers, laundry, a heated pool, and wifi. And you get to live in a state with no income tax. The prices farther south (in Naples) make Tampa look cheap.
  14. @bertvermeer5605: Is west coast live-aboard expense that much different than the east coast? I suspect that in the NE winter liveaboard is not really that practical.
  15. @rickbarrionuevo5270: My E46 is in Shoreline in Long Beach and the slip just went to $992, non-live aboard. The extra fee for living about is $150-200 for a couple.
  16. @Morrisfactor: I can't imagine California boaters bringing their boats to the Pacific Northwest to find moorage. California moorage costs are cheaper in comparison to the marina costs in the Puget Sound area. I was paying $870 a month for a 50' run down marina slip three years ago and that was well below market cost of the main marinas (Shilshole, Des Moines, Elliot Bay). By law, marina liveaboards are not allowed over 10% of total slips (and if they do, it's another $250 per month per person or more). Most small marinas don't allow liveaboards anyway.  Insurance is mandatory at all marinas and yards. Waiting lists for liveaboards at major marinas are often close to ten years! There are virtually no slips for boats 45' and up; the majority of vacant slips are for boats under 35'. Ecologists have stalled new marina building - the last one was Elliot Bay Marina - completed in 1991 only after 17 years of development/permit planning was done - effectively killing any chance that another such project can ever be accomplished.
  17. @JohnPetersen-jl4jx: We’re in a 50’ slip at Channel Islands Marina in Oxnard. No doubt about it, the expense of our beloved sailboat has for the last 3 years exhausted our retirement savings and destroyed my lifelong passion of sailing and boat ownership. Truly  a luxury only for the very wealthy nowadays. If I could do it all over again I’d get a trailerable folding trimaran. Best bang for the buck for sailing and cruising any day.
  18. @crazy4dariver: Lol and Ensenada is 18 a month up to 68 ft. My slip in MDR was 1400 with electric and water. No live on that was 400 more per person 2 max. Ensenada is 40 more and 10 per pet
  19. @robertbruce730: Don't forget that you have no garage for your car and gear etc. plus the salt air is tough on your vehicle.
  20. @daviddoyle6746: Note: many marinas will not allow live aboards on boats less than 36 ft. with some requiring even longer and most/all require $500,000 liability insurance ( just liability is not too bad, but many will not insure boats over 30 years old) and many/most will not insure wood boats at all.
I have never known of a marina asking for a deposit to be on a live aboard waiting list.

- The Ottisons:  A True Nantucket Story: Kind: captions Language: en foreign [Music] stepping on to this property is like stepping back in time the otisons myself my brother and my father built all these structures has been all constructed basically of Salvage wreckage from the shores and Nantucket we did the best we could with what we had we were approached way back what were we going to do with the property developers started knocking on our door they could make us very wealthy and we weren't about to leave the house we created the land we created that was never going to be in our lifetime we want to protect Nantucket because it's a really special place we've seen the best in Nantucket it's sad to see how much it's changed I think the old Nantucket is long gone my name is Susan Chase odesson I am a 10th Generation native nantucketer my name is Carl otisin and I'm a fourth generation native nantucketer we've been married 43 years oh hell no [Laughter] we're friends before we were born Carl and I were classmates forever and wasn't until our 10th class reunion we got together he says you go and I said yeah I guess I gotta go and he says so we went together figured there'd be no place to park if everybody took their own car so 196 1968 Parkin was not a real problem around here then growing up on Nantucket as a child was very easy to do foreign it was such a quiet place well growing up here was only 3 000 people you just had small groups of people that you hung out with we rode our bicycles we went places and picked berries and fishing and we may do it what we had and what we could grow in the garden growing his houses in the 1940s the property looked a lot different than what it does today farming was a way of life dairy cows horses Grayson in the meadow our family owned approximately 100 acres in which today covers from the rotary to Fairgrounds Road right to The Creeks the property we are on to today this property is very spectacular all the birds and wildlife it's crazy I mean it's just there's something out there all the time to watch Mother Nature just is a show in itself it's always changing it's always changing what's that um pink flower right there so is that uh where are you looking honey Lalo my mom and dad were married in 1940. my father was Albert k odeson my dad was a carpenter Builder very skilled woodworker he would make furniture homes boats so I would see him do those things I wanted to do those things and I spent the rest of my life trying to catch up to his skills which was no easy task yeah every aspect of building a house is something we did as a combined family effort my family is always taken a lot of pride in recycling materials and wood don't waste anything The Artisan family have always been into early recyclable stuff it's amazing how Mr otisin bad just would pick up a piece of wood and he would make a whirly gig he made his own nails when he built his boat I mean to me that's amazing the beam in the kitchen is the Mast from a ship that foundered off of Tucker Nook so we brought Jacks and rollers and we got it up so we could move it and push it down to the water's edge because it was a long ways from the water and we got it to float and we towed it back to madiquette and put it on a big trailer brought it home we did our own plow Moon we did our own design work and assembling it makes no difference whether it was a boat or a building or what have you The Sawmill on the property is something I put together from just mechanical pieces I had laying around I guess I always knew I wanted to build a sawmill it's the only family owned and operated Sawmill on the island people know that we like to have wood well sometimes they want to patch up a fence or a shot they don't want to put brand new boards up they want it to blend in where I guess the recycling unit for the island I mean we have a lot of very fussy customers and they don't want just everything brand new all the time so they come here this is our firewood supply for the coming winter Albert and I my brother here he uh we we run both our homes on on firewood furnaces all you need is a handful of newspaper and a match it's easy there's no cleanup you just use it step out and you're through solar hot water heater Outdoors for outside showers that's yourself box painted black with some black two inch holes in it heats the water up and I pushed the hot water out with cold water so the cold water is pushing the hot water into my shower stove we were doing these things before people become energy conscious it's a way of life on Nantucket when I graduated out of high school I didn't know what I was going to do with myself and I fell into a position of making Nantucket light chip baskets with somebody and he retired and I kept on doing it I've been doing it for 54 years it's fascinating I love working with my hands when Carl and I got married we created a workshop downstairs in the lower half of the house and we have a little retail area and and people would come and see actually baskets being made and the finished products were there and it became a in full-fledged business for us and everything is made right here from scratch in all the years that we've been doing we didn't never elaborate on who we're making baskets for so I finally kind of said from the White House on down and so I leave it at that I still make a few baskets for people that have had an order with me I'm trying to clean it up because I am retiring I don't put too much in here because the crows come and eat it so all right we're going to the pond well when I moved in back in 78 the bachelor pad that long ago yeah it was just a field of poison ivy and wild roses and I got itchy to get something going and preserve and open it up Susan expanded the lawn by about 900 percent when we first started I mean I had to go get books on what were the wildflowers growing here and my thought was to plant amongst the natural wildflowers that are already here some of the large willow trees on the east side of the lot I got the cuttings from damaged trees The Willows at the Harbor House had a we had a big storm and there were broken branches everywhere so Sue and I were riding around and I jumped out and I threw in a bunch of the branches that the crews hadn't picked up yet and I brought them home and stuck them in the ground various places around the lot they're down there now and they're they're probably over 30 feet tall they're 42 year old trees it would help to start it was the apple trees we wanted to get to those apple trees and see what else was around in the in the property Sue decided that we would get married under the one of the one of the closer apple trees so we made a path down to that and that was the beginning of paths this apple tree is at least 100 years old but this is the tree that we got married under in 1979 and we had just planted this rhododendron which was teeny weeny and it's huge now the early things that caught my interest in my wife was she invites me to dinner one night and she's cooking roast beef she put together a wonderful little dinner for the two of us and but she cooked that roast beef in a toaster oven with very minimal kitchen facilities she pulled that off flawlessly and I said to myself this is a very capable woman I worked on Yuzu Tommy of course he happened to come with a very beautiful home and a very beautiful view So I Married it we were approached way back in 2000 what were we going to do with the property we have no children so what are we going to do with this and and we hadn't really thought about it and this gentleman had some other partners and they were going to go in together and buy the whole eight Acres that we had they offered a surprise and right and we got that got us thinking wait a minute that's not the kind of thing we want to do we want to leave it for everybody not see it chopped up or hotel down along the road we just decided that we lived here for the rest of our natural lives and uh in the end it can go back to the people of Nantucket leaving this property in their hands it's going to show a lifestyle that we lived over there will bring a little joy to people like it has brought to us the otisin family

So everything is an open book and everything's on the table at this point, but you're going to rely on the autopsy once that's done for sure. >> When you interview someone and just from the Daily Mail reporting that the the boyfriend was naked at around midnight when all this happened and he's screaming for help. How do you go about these interviews when you're trying to figure out whether something criminal happened? >> Well, you want information. So, you have to be sympathetic to what's going on in his life. You know, if he had nothing to do with this, he's going to be broken. He's going to be upset. He's going to be emotional. So, you have to, you know, take that in into consideration in taking your time with an interview. Get them in a secluded area, sit them down and just take your time and methodically go through their relationship, what happened that night, what led up to this. So, you want to start at whatever beginning you can come up with, which will then lead to actually the end of this and and her death and and the 911 call, but you just really want to take your time with this. And the other thing is, you know, at some point you you are going to have to maybe start thinking of, you know, the future of this and a trial and a, you know, part of the criminal justice system. So, you want to make sure statements are written down the right way. Any Miranda warnings that are that have to be given are given. So, you want to make sure nothing's missed because you don't know what's going to take place with this or what's going to come up in the future. So, you start preparing for a prosecution or a trial immediately with making sure you do everything right. And this happened Tuesday night, Monday night into Tuesday. So, around midnight at some point is when the first calls to 911 were made. Now, we're heading into Wednesday afternoon. Not a lot of information from law enforcement as of yet. What type of timeline typically is there to giving the public more information about what happened? >> Well, you want to be sure in your information. You don't want to just have a press conference, and this happens way too often. You don't want to have a press conference for the sake of having a press conference because that just gets, you know, the information out there that doesn't need to be out there. And the investigators and whoever might be running this investigation at the time, you've seen it so many times. They're going to sit up there and say, "We're not going to comment on the investigation at this point." Which means, you know, they may have a break. They may have information on something that they're not willing to give out yet. And when all that is ready to, then they will. But, you know, we see it too often where press conferences, you know, are just done because they are, you know, and it's time for one. And maybe they're just holding on a little bit longer for this one to make sure they don't present that and everything's given to the press and the public the right way. No. And I'm guilty of that in being a member of the press is when uh the press conference, we want to see it happen fast and then we start asking questions and then when we get no comment, we're like, why are we here for this press conference? And and I know thing things don't work that quickly. Um, when I talked to the Suffach County Police today, they said you don't really know an ETA for an autopsy, but typically what goes into that process and how long it takes to get the results back in something like this. >> Well, just the autopsy itself can be done pretty quick. The toxicology report is is what takes the most time. That takes that could be actually a couple of weeks uh in order to get all that information and you know from that report. But the autopsy itself, as far as just a path pathology report, can be done pretty quick. And if there's no signs of anything involving her death with that, then we're going to have to wait for the uh the other report, which could take a couple of weeks. >> I I think this is the it builds itself as the largest marina in the Hamptons, which is saying a lot. That's all it is. these detectives in Suffach County and whoever else might be helping them out has been through this many many times and you run it just like any other investigation. You have your your case officer, case detective who's going to basically run the investigation and then the team and other people who are involved in this are just going to supplement him. They're going to go out do the interviews and then you collect all the uh the statements and you just start putting a a puzzle together and a timeline together. But it's a lot of coordination. Um and and for the reason being like she had thrown up a lot, it just seems a like there's something missing there and I don't know what I would do in that situation if you have someone you're with and then they die and then you're trying to figure out what happened like maybe they were dating. It's possibly an overdose according to the New York Post, but we haven't learned of what the toxicology results are and what the official autopsy found after the the initial one came back inconclusive, but the family says they did this second autopsy. Um, what typically goes into that when a family is able to get their own kind of examination of the body? >> So, you have to think that everything has been removed once they do that first autopsy. And so the the task of the second autopsy is to put all those things back together. So they have to look at the brain, but they have to they essentially are pulling them out of this big organ bag because all the organs are usually placed into a biohazard organ bag. So they have to remove all of those organs, identify all of those organs, and take their own samples of each. Typically in an autopsy, brain, liver, and kidney samples are taken. And then they'll also take samples, especially in a case like this where it's a suspected overdose. They're going to be taking a look at the adrenal glands. They they may take portions of the liver, the spleen, the pancreas. They may go ahead and take little portions of all of those organs to perform more uh testing, postmortem testing on them just to see if there's something that is not in alignment with the story that is being told. And I will tell you, this case feels like a a suspense novel, and we just have more questions than answers. I'm so um anxious to see what the final autopsy results reveal. And sometimes they can be different, which is really interesting. >> Yeah. Why is that? Because there are some cases we've seen where you'll have uh you know, a coroner's office in a part of the country. They come out, they may rule someone's death to be um we've seen cases where they rule it that someone had taken their own life. I mean, gosh, you look at the the Greenberg case out of Philadelphia. I mean, that's something that people have talked about where you have an office saying this is how this person died, but then you have a family that does their own private autopsy or their own investigation and they say the results are actually different and it becomes this um kind of debate back and forth about what happened. How is it that you can have a second autopsy that can find something very different from what the official autopsy finds from a coroner's office? So the reason for that is that the the cause of death is the medical opinion of the person doing the examination as to why they believe that someone died and those things can vary sometimes. Now sometimes you'll have a case like what you talked about with the Greenberg case where you have such a difference in opinion but what information do they have that backs up their cause of death? And that's what's going to be really important here. What information does the medical examiner have from the investigation, from the reports that were written, from the witnesses who were there, from the toxicology, from the last known moments when she was alive? Did she text someone prior to her death? Did she get on social media prior to her death? What was her disposition? How was she acting? Did she seem cognitively um despondent? Like, what things can we put together to make this make sense? And sometimes things don't make sense. So, if you have a case where someone had a possible drug overdose, but they have no known use of drugs in their entire life, this is where conversations with her friends become important. Hey, listen. We just want to find out what happened to her. Did she ever do cocaine? Did you ever see her partake in in any drugs? That's going to be important. And then on the other side, for the second autopsy, you could have someone saying, "This was a trace amount. It wasn't enough to cause this type of a reaction. we don't believe that drugs were the case at all. Maybe they were contributo to what was already going on with her, but they may not be the official cause of death. So, yes, you can have two very different opinions as to how someone died because again, it's just a medical opinion. >> And when it comes to I mean, to this point, again, it's just we only know what the family's autopsy found. We don't know what what Suffach County's found yet. But, um, you often hear about fentanyl with overdoses. I mean, how profound is that in someone's body if they ingest fentanyl? if it was something that might have been and and we don't know in this case, but even in a trace amount of cocaine, if there's fentanyl involved and that triggers an overdose, how profound is that in someone's body when you examine it? >> So, when you have fentinel of any amount, it depresses the respiratory system. And again, you have to look at the tolerance of the drug in someone's body. So, multiple things can impact that. So, if they were drinking, if they had other medications in their system, over-the-counter or or prescribed medications, what other things were present in the presence of this trace amount of cocaine? Because if you are taking a stimulant like drug and then you add cocaine to it, it could cause your heart to have an arhythmia. It's still in the body. So even if you did cocaine two or three days ago, if there's still trace amounts of it in your body and you take another medication, you can cause an adverse reaction and potentially a fatal reaction. Fentinil is about 200 times more powerful than morphine. It's an opioid uh analesic and it is powerful. So it's prescribed in micro dosages. So, if you take a small amount of fentinil, even if it's mixed with cocaine, which we often see in drug overdoses, just the tiniest amount, smaller than the size of a penny, could cause someone to have a fatal reaction because literally the body forgets to breathe. >> Wow. And um just lastly, with this just being such a mystery to this point, is there ever a case when a toxicology comes back, final autopsies done, that it's still inconclusive and we don't know how someone died? >> Absolutely. That's why we have the determination of undetermined because sometimes you can have all of the pieces of the puzzle put together and none of it makes sense or you have a manner of death that could be equally likely um across the four. So you have natural, accidental, suicide, homicide, and undetermined. The reason we have the undetermined category is because usually you can't choose between three. We know this was not a natural death. So now we have to make a determination between homicide, accident, and suicide. And no one has indicated that this is a possible suicide. So now we have homicide and accident. What was going on with this guy that she was with? Who else was there? And what were her final moments that led to her death? very important to get the statements from those people >> and and we'll see what happens. When we're left with testing the blood and the urine and the bile and the liver for drugs and chemicals, first you have to collect all of that material and then you have to send it to the laboratory. Everything is by chain of evidence and chain of custody and so you have to be careful with it because that's going to be important if anything comes of it. And then the very first screening has to happen where you're just looking at broad classes of drugs. Are there any opioids present? You know, uh are there any alcohols present? The Jerry Dunphy Shooting (1975)
In October 1975, the legendary Los Angeles news anchor was shot, but it occurred in the KABC-TV parking lot in Hollywood.

The Incident: As Dunphy and his makeup artist, Mary Lou Gallegos, were walking to his car after a broadcast, they were ambushed by two men.

The Injuries: Dunphy was shot in the neck and arm.

The Outcome: He survived the attack and eventually returned to the anchor desk, but the event was not captured on film or broadcast live.

- Why is Lake Tahoe so clear?: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Lake to is so clear because we have a granite Bedrock here in the Basin which doesn't break down into fine particles that can reduce the clarity of the lake we also have a watershed where a lot of water falls directly onto the lake so we don't have a lot of runoff and then we have a a system that's naturally nutrient poor so it's not feeding a lot of growth of algae one of the greatest threats to Lake to's Clarity is stormrunner pollution we've paved over a lot of our meows and wetlands with roads parking lots so now when storm water runs off it carries fine sediment pollution into Lake Tahoe Community members volunteering on our pipe Keepers Program help us identify problem pipes and advocate for solutions to storm water pollution be a part of the solution get involved in our pipek Keepers program and attend one of our trainings together we can keep Tahoe Blue

- The Hidden Threat to Lake Tahoe: Kind: captions Language: en I think the biggest misconception that people have about Lake Tyler that is that everything's perfect. I mean, it's hard not to think everything's perfect when you look around here, but it's actually that beauty that hides the threat. My name is Jesse Patterson. I'm the chief strategy officer for Keep Tahoe Blue. And I've been fortunate enough to be working to protect this lake for the last 13 years. And it's a dream job, greatest job in the world. I get to use all my passion and everything I enjoy doing, but do it in a way that makes sure we protect it for future generations. The blue of Lake Tahoe means everything. Without it, none of us would be here. And actually, the bless reflects how clean and pure the water is, cuz the clear it is, the deeper blue it gets. So, when I come here and I see this blue pure water, I know that I'm in my happy place. We've been aware of the threat of aquatic invasive species for several decades now, but mostly we thought they were only coming in on motorized boats. And just recently, we've realized with the introduction of New Zealand mud snails and the threat of golden muscles on our doorstep that they can get in on non-motorized boats, which means all the things we like to play with out at Lake Tahoe, from fishing gear to wet suits to paddle boards to floaties. So, every time you get in or out of the water, even just on something as simple as a beach walk or just playing, you could accidentally introduce something and that'll turn that clear water green and then that'll spread into the center of the lake. So, Keep Tahoe Blue created a program in 2013 called Eyes on the Lake that looks to get ahead of that. It's protect while you play. Come out, have fun on the water, but there's a couple easy steps you can take to make sure you're not accidentally introducing that next invasive species. So, when you're out on a boat or out deep in the lake and you see something floating around out here that doesn't look right, we actually like you to pick that up, take a photo of it with your smartphone, and send it to us on our Eyes on the Lake app, so we can make sure that those invasive species aren't spreading all over the lake. We created a wall of bubbles or a bubble curtain across the two channels to the Tahoe Keys, which are ground zero for aquatic invasive species spreading into Lake Tahoe. And we've been testing these bubble curtains in the channels of the Taho Keys since 2018, looking at different mechanisms and different ways to stop the weeds, but allow the boats so we can protect while we play. And the beauty of science is it can show you problems before you can see them with your own eyes. Cuz by the time you see green, nasty water or a bunch of invasive species taking over the beach. Undoing that's nearly impossible. We do eyes on the lake trainings where we invite the public and volunteers and employees at different marinas to come on in so we can teach them how to identify invasive species. This program is focused on the fun of Lake Tahoe while you are also focusing on the importance of protecting Lake Tahoe which is really great. Protect while we play is the Eisen Lake slogan which I think embodies it very well. The interactions can go a variety of different ways. is I would say the average is people when you approach them are very curious. They're like, "Okay, who are you? Why are you talking to me?" And once you kind of break down that barrier of, "Hey, you're not in trouble." But here's something that you can do to help keep Lake Tahoe beautiful. Definitely bring their guard down, which is wonderful. I think a lot of people who recreate here want to do the right thing, but they don't always know what that means, especially when it comes to invasive species. It's a very unknown threat, which is why we are out here talking about it. When you tell people what clean drain dry is, what invasive species are, they're very open and willing to help. Every time you're playing on the water, every time you're floating in the river, just do that. Clean, drain, dry, and it goes a very, very long way to protecting the lake. >> The most important thing you can do to protect Lake Tahoe forever is think about your individual impact and how to lessen that. And at Keep Taho, we make that easy by making the right choice the easy choice. So you can come here and have fun hiking, skiing, boating, whatever you love to do here, knowing it's done in a way that will protect it for future generations.

And by the time you're a senior, a first class midshipman called a firstie. You are responsible for the entirety of everybody underneath your command, their performance and their wellbeing. The brigade has a very clear structure to it. There's one brigade of midshipmen, that is everybody from there where it's split into two regiments. Each regiment has three battalions for a total of six, and each battalion now has six companies for a total of 36 companies. Within a company, you have four platoons. Each has a platoon commander. Within a platoon, you have three squads. And that is really our smallest defined unit, is a squad. No more than 12 midshipmen with a senior squad leader. You have some fire team leaders, some youngster mentors, and then of course the plebes. - Companies are like the best part about the Naval Academy. They become your family over the course of four years. You stay with the company that you're in for the rest of your time at the academy, and you train with them.
