Well, I'm here with Carlos, who's a cobbler at the Naval Academy. Been working here for over a decade. If you had to guess, how many pairs of shoes do you think you repair here a year? - Re-sole about 3,000 or so. - 3,000. - And I see all of these machines everywhere. - [Carlos] Yes. - Can you touch on a few of them? Well, we got the heel machines over on this side. We got the interstitial on that side and the press over there. We got the main machine, the finisher over there in the front. - And what we haven't mentioned yet is, how many people are on your staff? - Me, just me. - Just you. - Well, I'm sure the entire brigade is super appreciative of all that you do. - I hope so 'cause I try to do the best I can do for 'em. You know, if they need me, I'm here. - Now in addition to being a training ground US Navy and Marine Corps, every single midshipman who throws their hat in the air on graduation day also receives a Bachelor of Science degree. Academics play a vital role and both the curriculum and the academic facilities are what we're gonna check out next. - We have small class sizes, we have a ton of resources to help our students to succeed here. And everyone here at the Naval Academy is about the people. Everyone is focused on the development of the young men and women here to be future leaders for our nation. - The professors here are so willing to help you. Everybody wants to see you succeed. Yes, the academics are hard, but the teachers here work so diligently to make sure that you understand the material that you're learning. - Our academic program is largely divided into three schools. We have a School of Humanities and Social Sciences, the School of Mathematics and Science, and the School of Engineering, Computing, and Weapons. And then we also have a Department of Leadership Ethics and Law that has classes and a Department of Seamanship and Navigation. - [Student] One, five, zero. - Many benefits to the education here. The free tuition is definitely the first thing that certainly the parents think about when they're thinking about the Naval Academy. It's actually a step beyond the free tuition because they're paid when their student's here and they have a guaranteed job. So we hire all of our graduates here, but even while you're here, you're getting a skillset that really translates well into any future career. And our graduates, even if they don't choose to stay in the service beyond their service obligation, are very successful in finding jobs - Now hold up just a second because I wanna thank SoFi for sponsoring the next section of this video. As a veteran-led organization, SoFi proudly supports both current and former military members with their financial goals. I've actually been using SoFi myself for the last couple of years and have had nothing but good experiences with them. If you've served in the military and have considered buying a home, you've probably heard of the VA loan, a benefit most military members are aware of, but don't know exactly how it works, myself included. That's where SoFi's VA home loans come into play. They'll help walk you through the entire process, making it easier for you to qualify for lower interest rates with zero down payment, will help you save on fees, and will ensure you have access to a dedicated mortgage loan officer to answer any questions that you have. Now what I'm most excited about is that SoFi is giving away up to $50,000 to help pay the rent or mortgage of one of our military heroes for one year. I mean, that's pretty sick. Now to enter, you can click the link down in the description below or head to sofi.com/WinVA and follow all onscreen instructions to view your VA home loan mortgage rate. It takes just minutes to enter and explore your options and it won't affect your credit score whatsoever. So with that, a big thanks to SoFi. And now, let's get back to the video. (upbeat guitar music) Welcome to the Naval Academy's Robert Crown Sailing Center, home to what I think is one of the coolest classes midshipmen get to take during their time here. So this is the Naval Academy after all, and people wanna know, do they actually learn to sail while they're here? - Absolutely. So during plebe summer, every midshipman comes to the Naval Academy before the academic year to start what we call plebe summer. And during that time, they learn how to march, drill, and everything else to include learn how to sail. So they spend 12 hours on the water in our boats, learning basic points of sail, how to attack, how to jive, and they learn that from midshipmen who are most recently graduated from the Naval Academy. - And there's a lot of crew fields in the Navy. Some might not actually go to be on ships, but why is it important to start with that foundation and learn how to sail while they're here? - So our theory is if every marine is a rifleman, every sailor should be a sailor. So we teach them basic fundamentals of how to sail and it's life skills that they can take with them. It's followership, how to take orders. - Now while the sailing center provides an introduction to seamanship, the maritime opportunities midshipmen have doesn't stop there. The Naval Academy also operates a division of 108 foot yard patrol craft known as YPs. These vessels provide at-sea training in a realistic environment, teaching midshipmen naval tactics, communications, and other maritime procedures, which many will then go on to use upon entering the active duty fleet after graduation. So there aren't many colleges where part of your curriculum is actually going underway on a ship like this. For those who are watching and haven't experienced that, what is that like? - It's honestly one of my favorite parts of the Naval Academy. I remember my plebe year going out on the water and I was always so excited to be able to do that and come out here and actually get experience out on the water. - Now one thing I learned quite quickly during my visit to the Naval Academy is that traditions play a massive role in both the midshipman experience and the morale of the brigade. Just take the Beat Army culture, for example, something that around here is a big deal. Can you talk about the Beat Army culture here at the Naval Academy? - [Announcer] Here's what we've been waiting for, Carter. And here come the midshipmen of Navy from Annapolis. And from West Point, the Black Knights of Army. - Army is our closest friend, 364 days a year. - [Sam] And that one day? - That one day is the Army-Navy game, and then all bets are off and game on. - [Announcer] And the FA-18 Super Hornets overhead and the Chinook and Apache helicopters out with their team. - The Beat Army culture is infused in everything we do. Beat Army! - So when midshipmen comes how many times a day they should say beat Army. - In the end, we have, you know, teams that become national champions, but in the end, if you didn't beat Army, it's still considered a losing season. - We do an extra pushup to beat Army, we do an extra sit-up to beat Army, and it's a rivalry that just goes since our founding. That's who we wanna beat every year in every sport. - So it's definitely clear that traditions play a huge role. However, one of the most time honored traditions is one we've yet to talk about, and it happens at lunchtime. It's called noon meal formation, where the entire brigade assembles in front of Bancroft Hall to march together to eat. - [Leader] Ready, execute! - [Group] Sir, you now have 12 minutes till noon meal formation! Brigades goes outside T-Court at 12:05! - So what you just heard is what's known as chow call, where the plebes line up on each side of the hallway and recite things like the time until the noon formation, the menu for the noon meal, the uniform of the day, and a lot more that I can barely make out. They start at 15 minutes out from formation and recite it every minute up until five minutes. Then it's hurry up and get out to the T-Court before the call to attention is made. (upbeat trumpet music) ♪ Baba bing, bada boom ♪ - Welcome to King Hall, the Naval Academy's 55,000 square foot dining facility that serves the brigade of midshipment for breakfast, lunch, and dinner. Every day, the team of cooks and wait staff serve 13,500 meals for the roughly 4,500 midshipmen seated at the 392 tables spread across the dining hall. - Everybody kind of funnels in through the two main doors, walks down the stairs, comes down the big stairs here, immediately knows where their company area is and where their table's gonna be. - So we just sat down, everything is very hectic, and apparently, they, eat, finish, get outta here, and onto the next classes in like 15 to 20 minutes. And crazy operation, very efficient, and a lot of hungry midshipmen. - And it's definitely enough time to be able to sit down, eat, have a little conversation with your squad or whoever you're sitting with, and then get right back out to whatever the next thing you need to do is for the day. - [Sam] So we've covered both academics and the importance of military training but there is one final pillar that remains crucial to the Midshipman curriculum. Welcome to Navy athletics. ♪ All eyes on the way I thought ♪ ♪ When I walk into the room ♪ ♪ Wind blows underneath the soles ♪ ♪ In my brand new pair of shoes ♪ - The physical education curriculum here at the Naval Academy is super important to the development of leaders. Being at the Naval Academy, we have three semesters of swimming, we have a combatives class, everybody has to box, everybody has to wrestle. And we also have a personal conditioning class in there as well. So it's well-rounded. Naval Academy has 36 intercollegiate sports. We range from everything with 36 and we have sailing, rifle, crew, triathlon. It's a great spot to be The physical readiness test, which we call the PRT, it's given every semester while you're here at the Naval Academy and you're expected to pass. The PRT here at the Naval Academy consists of a mile and a half run, two minutes of pushups, and a plank hold with a max score at four minutes and 20 seconds. - Now outside of the mandatory PE classes and intercollegiate teams, the Naval Academy also offers a dozen competitive club sports, including lacrosse and the midshipmen pistol team. There's also more than 70 extracurricular activities available for midshipmen to explore their personal interests, develop additional skills, and build connections with their fellow classmates. One of my favorites, The Masqueraders, an all midshipmen drama and theater club, and one of the oldest clubs here at the Academy. - Masqueraders were officially found in 1907. And so it's the oldest extracurricular activity here on the yard. But we trace our roots back to 1846 where an early performance happened in downtown Annapolis that actually was derailed after a little too much celebration. (Sam chuckling) - And so why is it important, I mean, to have a club like this on the yard? You know, people, I don't think Naval Academy think this is something midshipmen can do, but they can. - Right. No, I think it's a really important club because it gives them an outlet outside of their typical STEM technology-focused education to do something else, but also to explore the human side of being an officer and explore what other people's views are by enacting that on the stage. - Now what I think is probably one of the coolest perks of the Naval Academy is its proximity to Downtown Annapolis. In less than a mile, you'll find yourself in the heart of historic downtown with more restaurants and bars and activities than you can possibly imagine. (inspiring music) As you walk the cobblestone streets, you see Navy flags outside the houses, Navy alumni who've moved back and now call Annapolis home. It definitely feels like a college town. But one of the biggest traditions that takes place here in Downtown Annapolis happens before the start of every football season and I think perfectly sums up the relationship this town has with the Naval Academy. - To feel the community next to you and to see the way that they lined up to support the brigade of midshipmen was just so special. And that feeling of community around you and the energy and the excitement for the first home game, but also just to see the brigade of midshipmen (light piano music) - Welcome to Navy Marine Corps Memorial Stadium, home to Navy midshipmen football, and one of the most historic college football programs in the country. - Welcome to the Annapolis Maritime Museum: Kind: captions Language: en welcome to the Annapolis Maritime Museum your connection to the Natural History of the Chesapeake Bay and the cultural heritage of the hardworking men and women who make their living on it the museum is housed in the historic mcnasby Oyster Company building the last vestage of a major industry that once thrived in Annapolis as it did all along the Chesapeake Bay Annapolis has a unique Maritime Heritage in history before Captain John Smith sailed by 400 years ago on his Voyages of Discovery Piscataway and Susan Indians traveled through this area Hunting Fishing and Gathering oysters for food early English settlers grew tobacco and shipped it back to England to trade for everything they couldn't provide for themselves here including slaves in 1695 Anapolis was chosen to become the capital of Maryland because of its central location and its Snug Harbor up until the revolution Annapolis was a thriving Center of transatlantic trade but as Commerce increased much of that industry shifted to Baltimore while Baltimore boomed Annapolis remained relatively undeveloped that's why so many historic buildings survived into the 21st century when the United States Naval Academy was founded in 1845 local Commerce depended upon steamboats that connected Annapolis with other ports on both shores of the bay transporting passengers produce and other cargo with reliable speed on Dependable schedules after the Civil War the oyster industry boomed a record 18 million bushels were harvested in 1886 competition for the Chesapeake's white gold became so Fierce Maryland oyster police fought bloody battles with piratical watermen today over harvesting pollution and disease have reduced the oyster population to to a mere 1% of its historical level as the seafood industry declined new technology made recreational boating more accessible modern fiberglass power boats and sailboats have largely replaced traditional wooden workboats a year around schedule of rotas and events from the Wednesday night races to the world-renowned Annapolis boat shows has secured the city's reputation as America's sailing Capital the Annapolis Maritime Museum tells these stories of our unique history and Heritage through exhibits and programs the exhibit Gallery is the original shuing room an appropriate setting for the core permanent exhibit oysters on the half shell this exhibit tells about the bay once ubiquitous by Valve it's importance to the health of the Chesapeake Bay about the watermen who harvested them and the men and women black and white who shucked and packed Them In This Very Room the Buchanan Bay room serves as a maritime art gallery a concert hall for traditional and contemporary Maritime music and a lecture hall where authors historians and other experts share and explore the culture and Heritage of Annapolis in the Chesapeake Bay this space is also accessible for family corporate or Community celebrations and events the exhibit gallery and the Bayo also serve as classrooms for the Museum's education programs every year 2,000 students from schools in and around Anapolis get connected to the Bay its natural history and cultural heritage the museum provides rare public access to the Waters of the bay you can visit by boat or water taxi fish and crab from the peers launch a canoe kayak or tennis ball retriever from the beach catch a summertime Maritime concert or just watch the birds and boats Sail by in the summer the museum provides tours by boat out to the Thomas Point show Lighthouse a National Historic Landmark built in 1875 it's the last screw pile Lighthouse left in its original location 1 and a half miles off the mouth of the South River whether you're visiting for the first time or you set your anchor here for good you'll discover that this isn't just a great small Museum it's a focal point of community Spirit a place to gather and share our history Our Heritage by celebrating our past we can preserve our distinct identity and use it as a Compass to guide us on our course into the future we hope you enjoy your visit and come back throughout the year for one of our Maritime concerts lectures special events like the Annapolis oyster roast in the spring or the Boatyard beach bash in the fall and please join the crew become a member or volunteer as a museum dosent teacher tour guide Boatright Gardener aquarium keeper or event planner the museum is a site in the four rivers Heritage area this presentation was made possible by a grant from four rivers and the Maryland Heritage areas Authority the museum is also a site on the Captain John Smith Chesapeake National Historic Trail and the Chesapeake Bay gateways and water Trails Network

- Why the ocean is getting louder: Kind: captions Language: en This is me. I'm about to go scuba diving for the first time ever. I went in expecting muffled peace and quiet. I mean, one of the earliest documentaries on underwater life was literally called “The Silent World”. “They roamed deep under the Mediterranean, the Red Sea, and the Indian Ocean, in a mysterious realm, a silent world.” But as soon as I got down a few yards, I couldn't help but notice sound all around me. It was hard to tell what direction it was coming from, or how far away it was. It was coming from boats above me. As far as I can tell, the Earth’s water is not as quiet as I thought. I’ve been binging this podcast all about sound, and they've been looking into this too "From DeFacto Sound, you’re listening to Twenty Thousand Hertz: The stories behind the world’s most recognizable and interesting sounds. I’m Dallas Taylor." So Dallas — life underwater can be pretty loud, right? It can! Just listen to these toadfish— they oscillate their swim bladders to make sounds that are loud enough to keep houseboat residents in Sausalito, California up at night. Or these Barred Grunt fish, that make a grinding sound with the teeth in their gullet. Or these snapping shrimp — they produce a sound by creating tiny popping bubbles with their claws. It’s been measured at 200 decibels. That’s louder than a gunshot. Light doesn’t penetrate very deep in water, so a lot of ocean life has evolved to use sound as its primary sense. Because water particles are more densely packed together than air, sound travels farther and faster in water. That makes it an efficient medium of communication at any depth or time of day. “Let's say we had 440, like the middle key of your piano, or whatever.” That’s John Hildebrand, he’s a professor at the Scripps Institution of Oceanography. He's an expert in the field of underwater sound and how it’s used by and how it affects marine mammals. “If you looked at the wavelength of that sound in air, that note, the 440 note, would be a little bit less than a meter long. In water, it's more like several meters long.” What that means is that noise travels about four times faster and farther in water than it does in air. In an experiment in 1991, sound emitted from Heard Island was picked up at 16 sites in the Atlantic, Pacific, and Indian Oceans. It turns out that one of the most common noises in the entire ocean that can be picked up almost anywhere these days, sounds like this: It's the sound of boats. Noise from ship traffic has doubled every decade since the 1960s. “Basically, anywhere you go, the ambient noise in the ocean is dominated by anthropogenic sound.” “Let's say I made a measurement 30 years ago and now if I went to the same place and made the same measurement, it would be 10 dBs or more higher sound level than what I measured when I started my career. That’s spooky.” That’s a huge problem for animals that use sound as their primary sense. Just listen to this audio of how noise from a passing boat totally drowns out dolphin communication. But arguably the worst culprit of underwater sound pollution is a process that sounds like this: This is seismic surveying. It’s a process that allows companies to locate spots on the ocean floor where they can drill for fossil fuels. Boats with about 30-40 airguns that all go off at once will move back and forth over large parts of the ocean. Bubbles from the horns expand and contract typically every 10 seconds, creating a huge amount of acoustic energy that maps geological structures deep in the ocean floor. And it’s about as loud as a jet at takeoff. This can go on for weeks at a time. A study of seismic survey noise between 1999 and 2009 found that airgun sounds were recorded almost 2500 miles away from the survey ship itself. At some locations, they were recorded on 80 percent of days for over a year. And that changes how animals behave. A study by the Institute of Marine Science at the University of North Carolina found that reef fish abundance decreased by 78 percent during seismic surveying. This is what the reef looked like before seismic surveying in the area. And this is what it looked like after. “Seismic surveying is a constroversial topic at the coast and new research is only adding to the conversation. “One of those impacted is local fisherman Jack Cox. He’s seen firsthand the impact seismic surveying has on fish.” “It does something, that — we just don’t catch fish. For animals like whales — who rely on complex sound communication systems to socialize, find food, and mate — that poses a huge problem. If you watch this heat map from a study of endangered North Atlantic right whales, you can see that shipping paths near Boston squeeze the whales into a tiny space where communication is possible — it's about a third of what it was without the ships. Susan Parks, a biology professor at Syracuse, recorded right whale sounds throughout the early 2000s. And when she compared her audio to some that had been recorded in 1956, she noticed that the older sounds were much deeper than her high-pitched recordings. It turns out that the whales had started calling at higher and higher frequencies in order to hear each other over the hum of ship noise. Apart from habitat displacement and communication changes, there’s also evidence that boat noise simply stresses these whales out. After the September 11th attacks, researchers in Canada’s Bay of Fundy compared underwater noise levels during a period of reduced ship traffic to the stress-related hormone levels in the right whale population. They discovered a 6 decibel noise decrease in the bay after 9/11, which correlated to lower baseline levels of stress related hormones. Effects like this go all the way down the food chain. A 2017 study found that there were two to three times more dead zooplankton after after a day of blasts from a single airgun. And the larvae of krill, which whales rely on for food, were totally destroyed. In April 2017, Donald Trump signed an executive order to open offshore drilling in the Atlantic. It directed the Interior Department to consider allowing seismic exploration by 5 companies that had been blocked under the Obama administration. It’s getting widespread pushback in Congress — there’s a bipartisan House bill and Democrat-led Senate bill to ban seismic testing — but if those authorizations go through, companies could be conducting seismic testing in the Atlantic by Fall 2017. So… is there any good news? Yes, there is. In 2014, the International Maritime Organization adopted guidelines for reducing commercial ship noise levels with things like noise-muffling propellers and insulated engines — though they’re not mandatory yet. A year later, the US Navy agreed to limit sonar testing — which has been linked to whales stranding themselves on beaches in habitats near Southern California and Hawaii — following a lawsuit from environmental groups. And in June 2016, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration laid out an Ocean Noise Strategy Roadmap. It’s the start of a 10-year plan to assess the human impact on underwater sound and the measures required to reduce it. Seismic surveys can work better, too. Norway has started multi-client surveys, so that seismic data from a particular area is only collected once. US authorities are considering a similar process. The ocean is huge. We’ve explored less than 5 percent of it so far. So it’s easy to forget that what we do on the surface affects everything down below. But like plastic, chemicals, and waste, noise pollutes our oceans. Understanding that is key to doing our part to protect it. Thank you so much for watching, we loved doing this collaboration with Twenty Thousand Hertz. They create super highly produced podcasts that tell stories all about sound. If you enjoyed this video, you should absolutely go check out their podcast on the exact same topic. You can find that and subscribe at applepodcasts.com/20k.

- The Gilded Age | What it was Like to Summer in Newport, Rhode Island: Kind: captions Language: en for the titans of the Gilded Age the place to be in the summer was not Fifth Avenue in New York or the Sham ciz in Paris instead the coveted summer address was along bellw Avenue in Newport Rhode Island to escape the heat wealthy families from New York Boston and Philadelphia chose Newport as the place to see and be scene the city by the Sea gained favor as a popular summer getaway due to its mild climate and million dooll ocean views here American millionaires sought to outshine each other at balls Banquets Carriage parades concerts and sporting events among many other entertainments Newport became the place to spend the summer for members of the 400 the rich and those social climbers who wanted to be accepted among the right people that was especially true after William backous Aster Jr and his wife Caroline purchased Beachwood in 1881 with that Newport became the place for the old money to Summer and the new money quickly followed in the 40-year period between 1870 and 1910 hundreds of large summer homes were built in what had long been a relatively quiet and elegant summer Outpost the whoo who of high society built spectacular summer Mansions modeled after European palaces the owners of these Grand Estates refer to them as Cottages but don't let the name fool you some had over 50 rooms and could accommodate large numbers of guests the Newport Mansions were built to make a statement about wealth and status and no expense was spared in constructing the palacial properties the architectural one upmanship included manicured Gardens extensive collections of art ornate ballrooms and the latest in modern technology electricity running water ice makers telephones and inh home intercom systems to call upon their servants when Cornelius Vanderbilt thei built the breakers his status as one of America's richest men was on full display with 48 bedrooms 50ft ceilings in the Great Hall bakara crystal chandeliers Platinum leaf on the walls and a music room that was built in France disassembled and shipped to the states to be reassembled set on 11 acres the house occupying an acre itself Richard mois Hunt built the grandest house in Newport and one of the grandest in the history of the US arriving guests entering the Great H looked directly toward a wall of glass overlooking the lawn and the Sea the house was inaugurated on August 14th 1895 by the coming out party of the Vanderbilt's daughter Gertrude 300 attended and the catian was led by Gertrude and her partner Liz paned Stewart who was regarded as the best dancer in Newport Crossways the grand White columned Mansion Where Mrs dcent fish scandalized turn of the century Society with such entertainments as her servants ball for which guests dressed up as footmen and skullery Maids Marble House a neoclassical Mansion began as a birthday present for Alva Vanderbilt in 1888 the home was constructed of 500,000 cubic feet of marble and Drew inspiration from the petite Triano at ver Marble House was built right next door to Alva's onetime Nemesis Mrs AER who occupied the 62 room Beachwood more than 2 million had been spent on construction and another 11 million on decor and interior during building ala had high walls constructed so that no one could see what was going up her move was not popular and the neighbors were in an uproar when it was finally visible as it was the most palacial house in Newport at the time and was right on bellw Avenue for all to see they called it a marble house for a marble heart the homes Gold Room is considered to be the most opulently decorated room in Olive Newport and was host to many balls and dances the Chinese Tea House on the grounds overlooks the seaside Cliffs cliffwalk a 3 and 1/2 Mile Scenic path runs along the coastline off bellw Avenue right at the foot of the summer cottages this public access was Troublesome to the residents early on as it intruded on their privacy in those days the public was just as intrigued by the social Elite as we are today so it was decided to sink cliffwalk by 12T or so so the Walkers couldn't see the houses they were traveling by only the sea The Elms may have been modeled after an 18th century French Chateau touting a sunken garden that evokes a Parisian domain but it was decidedly modern as one of the first houses in America wired for electricity with the largest Ballroom in Newport rosecliffe was built for entertaining and was home to some of the summer social Seasons sink Suare marrying the extravagance of the Roman Emperor Empire its summer residents spent Millions on entertainment for the 8 to 10 weeks of the Season it was not uncommon for the most affluent to set aside $100,000 to $300,000 or more for the season the ladies who were leaders in society commanded the large number of servants necessary for the upkeep of the Cottages the queens of American society such as Mrs Herman olrick Mrs St fish Mrs William back house AER and Mrs William K Vanderbilt were pay Setters and created a new standard for social entertaining each season a proper Newport Hostess to maintain her social standing would have to hold six dinner parties with 60 guests each and two balls with an invitation list of 600 each the girls got along fairly well although they all had very strong and dominating personalities one of the issues that could create friction was who got the prized men to always be present at their parties Society in Newport was very much a woman's game The Men Who made the money that allowed for all this social climbing and party giving stayed away from it for the most part they worked in the city during the week and came to Newport for the weekend via private railroad cars and steam yachts there were a few male clubs that were off limits to women as well as yachting a favorite for many and the husbands did dutifully show up at some of the dinners and balls but as for planning and playing the social game most men simply were not interested aside from one certain gentleman who made Newport a Bonafide hotspot of the Gilded Age in the 1850s Ward McAllister a lawyer bought a home in the area McAllister made the place famous for his picnics an invitation to one of his summer picnics could make or break a hopeful social climber guests included New York's most powerful families aers Whitney Vanderbilts wet Moores they picnicked alongside diplomats state and local political figures and even artists however McAllister once commented Do Not For a Moment imagine that all were indiscriminate asked to these little fets if you were not in the Inner Circle it took the combined efforts of all your friends backing and pushing to procure an invitation for you these exclusive summer picnics were held at Bayside Farm's rustic white farmhouse and pictures Livestock in the pastures were rented specifically for the occasion it created a romantic outof Town feeling that seemed like a novelty guests feasted on Rich delicacy and enjoyed champagne and wine from McAllister's meticulously curated collection afterwards there was dancing in the barn where a brilliant chandelier hung from the rafters between food decorations table settings music and other Essential Elements for hosting 140 people one of McAllister's picnics cost roughly $28,000 in today's money during the warmer months m frequently entertained his high-profile guests by the sea in Gilded Age Newport even a day at the beach was a prestigious occasion in the 1890s newly electrified trolley lines made Easton's Beach accessible to the general public to the dismay of its better healed patrons not wanting to associate with the masses newport's wealthy claimed a small strip at the far end of bellw Avenue as their own and formed the spouting Rock Beach Association better known as Bailey's Beach mornings were spent at the beach club where a woman sat arrayed listening to the strains of an orchestra it was maintained by beachmen dressed in white with rows of bath houses with initials painted on them identifying their owners Alva Vanderbilt could be seen carrying a green umbrella into the water with her to protect her Fair complexion it became the most exclusive place in the Newport Social Scene if you are invited to a bathing party at Bailey's you are definitely one of the chosen but exclusivity does not always equate to higher quality Bailey's Beach was not a pleasant place to swim it was small and cramped and because of its location was plagued by a heavy growth of seaweed just offshore the public beach was much larger more pleasant and seaweed free but if they had even the slightest concern for their reputation a member of the upper class would not be caught dead there everyone in Rhode Island had a much closer relationship with boats and the water during the guilded Age fairies were the transport of the day with only one small Bridge spanning the sakon river a quid neck Island on which Newport is located was largely cut off from the mainland except by sea but the water wasn't just used for travel the prestigious New York Yacht Club made Regular cruises through Newport and families and clubs sponsored individual races on naragon siip Bay that were often covered in the New York Times Ogden golet owner of ochre court and James Gordon Bennett Jr were among some of the era who sponsored sailing cups bearing their names the trophies were made by the best silver Smiths of the age including Tiffany and Co and the Goram Manufacturing Company of Providence the Yachts were in the harbor at Newport often only to pick up their owners who would then be off with Pals and parties on cruises that removed them from the Newport social scene for most Season as with any Leisure activity when the elite families of Newport took to the water to enjoy the popular pastime of yachting they wanted the best and in Rhode Island the bestman fine from the haris shof Manufacturing Company in Bristol the biggest names of the Gilded Age were harrishof customers and yting was as significant a means of displaying your wealth and privilege as coaching or having a magnificent Mansion built or racing horses tailgating was very much a part of the social and sporting scene during the Gilded Age and Newport was the home of the first Polo Club in the United States States it all started in 1876 when New York Herold publisher James Gordon Bennett Jr invited a group of high society Sportsmen for a polo demonstration at his estate by the end of the night a rail car of horses had arrived for the match and the new found Polo devotees were hooked the group founded the Westchester Polo Club and based their playing season in Newport where most had summer residences the club originally played at Morton Park on kajes Shaw Avenue later establishing formal Polo Grounds off Ocean Drive eventually the game started to catch on in other cities along the Eastern Seaboard the Westchester Polo Club was the first and became a founding member of the United States Polo Association the club lasted for several Generations but ceased to be active after 1929 another bellw Avenue hotspot was a playground for the city's Elite in 1879 the same James Gordon Bennett Jr bet his friend Captain Henry Augustus candy that he wouldn't ride his Polo horse across the porch of the Newport reading room the notoriously stuffy Social Club where both were guests candy raised the BET riding his horse not only across the porch but through the building and ride out the back door disturbing members along the way the guest privileges revoked Bennett resolved to open his own social club and the Newport Casino was born at least that's the story that's been passed down through the years in reality the businessman was already in talks with architectural firm McKim me and white about constructing a new Social Club when he purchased the parcel on bellw Avenue in 1879 construction was completed in just 6 months and on July 26th 1880 a soft opening was held with guest admittance by invitation only one week later on August 2nd the public was invited to inspect the premises and more than 3,000 attended far from the modern concept of a casino as a gambling institution originally a casino was a summerh house or social club likewise the Newport Casino was a social venue that included both public outdoor spaces and a private club the horizontal facade on Belleville avenue included storefronts gentleman's lodging and a restaurant on the ground floor with Club rooms reading rooms and Billards on the second and third floors Gables tall chimneys small windows and open air porches completed architecture the grounds were designed to include a court tennis facility bowling alley and Lawn Tennis Courts they would have concerts horse shows flower shows and once the automobile entered the scene car shows and then there was this new sport that had recently hopped across the pond called lawn tennis for its first 34 years the casino played host to what would eventually become the US Open the ladies went in the late mornings dressed to the nines to sit under their umbrella was and to watch the tennis matches on other days the casino hosted mixed doubles club play with men and women playing together Fabrics such as linen and wool hid perspiration and women often played in their corsets and long skirts in addition to tennis the club held balls concerts and social events in the Casino theater the seats of the theater could be removed allowing room for dancing the public for a small fee could purchase tickets to stand in the balcony and observe the elite parting down below archery Lawn Bowling tea parties croquet and squash were all part of the social offerings writing 3 days after the opening the Newport News boasted it is doubtful if a more lively place can be found the daily diversion at Newport was an afternoon drive or Co parade every day the ladies in their hor drawn carriages led by footmen drove down Bell Avenue waving and nodding at one another it was customary to dress in your best finery for this daily circuit between Wilderness and waves the ladies got around in a variety of carriages chosen especially for the occasion Mrs penbroke Jones could be seen in the early hours on the streets in her morning carriage Jones was from North Carolina and was famous in Newport for her Southern dinners spending a summer in Newport required the proper wardrobe there were different dresses for every occasion 80 to 90 in a season worn only once or twice from the 1890s up to the 1910s Vogue extensively covered Newport focusing on what to wear in 189 six they reported at Newport alone is this summer picture seen at its best set in residential gorgeousness and environed with the splendors of wealth one must look for what is smartest and find the models of Fashion's latest Capri the outside world takes Delight in these fine leaders and enjoys with pride being their followers what New Port woman do and wear is a law from east to west north to south so grass Lawns are the most Chic by far whether plain embroidered in white or pink blue yellow mauve or rought with marbles of Lace incrustations supplemented by many fine needle workk traceries and lavish use of lace edings summer pageantry was never quite so much on display as it was in Gilded Age Newport with many of the summer cottages saved by the preservation society and available for touring the the glittering social life of dinners Sports and parties are now forever kept Alive by the Magnificent buildings that once housed them

- Dr. Winslow's Heart, A Love Story about Coming Home to Nantucket: Kind: captions Language: en we were lucky enough to spend summers in quit nits each year in the old life-saving station also known as humane house number 61 summers in quid net were simple and fun we had free rein for adventures biking sailing swimming games on the beach and fishing this is me we caught a lot of blue fish an UGG from the next day my mother would make something called Luna fish which none of us liked this is a story about a story that we were told when we were children we always thought it was a ghost story but it turns out that it is really a love story about coming home to Nantucket the story of dr. Winslow's heart would usually be told to us while we were gathered around the fire on a dark Knight Charles F Winslow was born in a house on India Street in 1811 when the American flag had only 15 stars and James Madison was president when he died the flag had 38 stars and ulysses s grant was president in 1811 there were six thousand eight hundred and seven people living on Nantucket the Nantucket Enquirer was first published in 1821 the Great Fire wiped out most of town in 1846 in 1855 Dorcas honorable the last Native American Indian died and of course Nantucket was the whaling capital of the world dr. Winslow had a natural curiosity about the world he was trained by his loving mother Phebe Winslow in such a manner that he quickly outdistanced every other pupil in the Nantucket school in 1834 he graduated from Harvard Medical School he became a doctor a lawyer and an advanced student of astronomy and the nature of the universe he spent time in many far-flung places including the Hawaiian Islands Tahiti Chile and California he married Lydia coffin Jones and became one of the islands most prominent physicians after his wife's death in 1874 dr. Winslow made his will and it was unique I requests order and command that if in the course of eight and forty hours it be positively proved that I am dead my heart shall be removed from my body by a competent anatomist placed his strong glass vessel having a ground glass stopper and that this vessel be immediately filled for the purpose of embalming my heart the saturated solution of Murie eight of ammonia I order this vessel put in a thick oak box may Justice sighs and the Box saturated with coal tar this I wished enclosed in a plain pine case and buried in the south of new town burying ground on the island of Nantucket where I was born my dear father lies by my mother's side and a single stone records their final resting place thus made this sacred spot be known where I wish my heart to rest i order that my body be burned and placed in the same grave at Mount Auburn in Boston which contains the precious remains of my great dear and beloved wife signed dr. Charles F Winslow his instructions were carried out and there is evidence that he may have been the first person in the West to be cremated his ashes are buried with Lydia on mound Avenue at Mount Auburn in Boston under some very tall and old trees there are nine town owned cemeteries onion Tucket and they are just about completely full you might be familiar with some of them such as the Quaker burial ground which has over 5,000 Quakers in it stacked like dominoes but only 56 tombstones the Quakers did not believe in earthly bonds or frivolous ornamentation I often wonder what they would think of Nantucket today as children we would go visit dr. Winslow's heart when I fell backwards into a hole and there it was completely grown over I came back here I'm looking for City Council I found it it's buried under the ground Charles F Winslow you don't think we're gonna get arrested II [Music] the reason we know that the story is true is that Edward Rho snow a student of New England maritime history researched and wrote about it in the 1940s he came to the island to verify the story most of the Islanders he asked had never heard of dr. Winslow's Hart but enough had that he was able to investigate further he obtained permission to have the caretaker of the new town cemetery use an iron prod to poke around the ground at the foot of the parents grave and not too far down he hit a hard object that was the size of a small box they dug it up and opened it and inside was a smaller box holding the glass vessel with dr. - Marina del Rey Documentary: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] I would sit on the edge or in the cockpit of my boat and just look around and watch the water and just think about how beautiful this place was I there there was nothing there but just how beautiful it was with the water being so calm and it being warm and thinking this is really a a paradise that I was just living for the moment and the beauty and the the day each and every day was a glorious day that was good enough for me after the Civil War prominent railroad companies were heading out west in search of areas to set up ports at the time the West Coast was wide open and they were all hungry to expand their shipping range the aeson toeka and Santa Fe Railway considered the area of what is now Marina delr and P Del re to be a worthy place to build a large scale commercial Harbor the area provided a perfect portal to the domestic and international shipping opportunities they were after the company partnered with prominent real estate developer and former attorney Moses Langley Wicks Wix had already played a part in the development of Lancaster Glendale San Bernardino and many other Los Angeles communities and was looking to expand his reach in 1886 they created the bologa harbor and improvement company with a Capital stock of $300,000 to dredge out Port balona the plan was to turn a quiet Seaside Marshland into a major commercial Harbor to be named Port balona but it wasn't quite so simple Port biona was supposed to be big enough to house all the ships in the world it was going to be a very large Port but the dreads that Moses Wix had was inefficient physically they couldn't do it this dredging a harbor in here took uh gargantuan means or means that we didn't have available to us until the 1960s while the attempt was Valiant Wicked tides and winter rain swept the sand back into the channel almost as fast as a dredger scooped it out it couldn't get down through some bous or some clay mud about 10 ft below so they had trouble dredging finally when a major storm in 1889 carried away most of what was built the project was abandoned in 3 years of Relentless trying the Venture was scrapped and the area returned to local duck Hunters things were quiet for the next 12 years until 1902 when Moses Sherman and Eli Clark formed the beach Land Company this group group owned about a th Acres around the balona Lagoon including port balona and renamed the area pla Del re Beach of the king they sold lots and created a destination for day tourist but the area that would one day be the largest man-made Harbor in the world was still essentially Marshland the last attempt at a commercial Harbor was when millionaire Abbott Kenny met with the US us core of engineers in 1916 he believed the space would have better protection for fishing and Commercial small craft than Los Angeles Harbor he also asserted that the location was Superior by virtue of its proximity to downtown Los Angeles no federal action was taken at the beginning of the Great Depression in 1929 the Ohio oil company brought in a wildcat well east of what is now the marina Peninsula they started drilling Wells on which were now plot plann 30 foot Lots on both sides of what is now the Silver Strand at a time of unprecedented economic suffering the vast majority of local homeowners demanded that zoning laws be Chang to allow oil extracting equipment on their lawns this area was um uh littered with oil wells and oil production material beach houses in Venice right on the beach would have oil wells in their side yards uh everybody wanted an oil well the beach was filled with dcks in 2 years there were almost 150 oil wells in production at full strength what was then known as the pad Del re field produced almost 47,000 barrels of oil and 2 million cubic feet of gas daily when the oil field reached 340 wells in 1931 with 38 different companies on 10 acres it became the fourth most productive oil field in the state by early 1932 the area was nearly depleted everybody was greedy they're putting more oil wells down getting oil and the pressure diminished of course and so this started in the late 20s and went through about 19 45 about I remember an editorial in the heral express that said stop this Forest of Derek while many local residents and companies made money during this miniature Boom the environment took a beating what was once a pristine Coastal Paradise was looking more like an oil stained dump it became a noisy smelly dangerous oil field that was no longer earning its keep after the oil production started to Peter out uh they polluted the land so much that it was uh impossible to be used as residential land the actual area of the marina was so polluted they they couldn't really do anything and so as worthless land it was a lot either easier to assemble it and into one coherent block and to develop it it was mosquitoes who enjoyed this dank dirty place next to the Blue Pacific more than anyone else it eventually became infested with the bugs and Los Angeles County was forced to create an abatement plan to protect residents from what was essentially a local Scourge the area was ugly and the abatement costly so by the late 30s politicians began to reexamine what could be done with a failed attempt at a commercial Harbor in the distant pass and a catastrophic flooding disaster of bologa Creek in 1933 the notion of turning the site into a substantial recreational Harbor began to take shape local Advocates wrote to congressman John Dockweiler who eventually introduced a bill to establish a harbor at pad Del Rey Inlet the regional Planning Commission submitted a report in 1938 entitled Marina del re this report emphasized the harbor would service the recreational needs of an already large and growing number of Voters in the area prior to World War II proposals were exchanged on a federal level discussing the feasibility of such a harbor in the pad Del re area after the war these discussions began Again by the late 40s local Advocates like Rex Thompson and Arthur will began the push to transform the former oil field into a modern Harbor in community with President Dwight heisen Hower now at the Helm of the Country Transportation related issues were considered with more seriousness the Army Corps of Engineers had earlier created an underwater wall alongside bologa Creek built for more effective drainage of the city un intentionally the wall created a deeper body of water where the present Marina sits Lake Los Angeles or Mud Lake as it was known became a recreational hotspot for small sailboats and skib boats in the 1950s and was a preview of what the future would hold in 1954 President Eisenhower signed Public Law 780 that allocated 7,738 th000 to the construction of the paa Del re Inlet that would later be renamed Marina del re in December of 1957 ground was broken and construction began in what was to be the world's largest recreational Harbor it aimed to birth 8,000 pleasurecraft and serve a growing Los Angeles population through the early 50s and on into the 60s politicians like Rex Thompson and Burton Chase were worked tirelessly with state and federal officials to ensure the project became a reality Chase often called the father of the marina is said to have understood the business element of Marina del re with great clarity he was committed to ensuring that the foundational economic premises be adhered to that the marina be a solvent money generating entity for the County of Los Angeles the first boats arrived in 1962 and Marina Del Rey was a reality while the harbor was carved out in contracted topc what Army Corps Engineers didn't realize was that the natural topography combined with the new man-made topography caused powerful surges during times of Stormy Weather specifically what happens is in a Marino like this the docks float uh to accommodate for the tide so that the the dock and the boat are at the same height to one another and they keep the docks in place by on pilings and so that dock is free to float up and down guided and kept in place on these pilings when they did that in Marino Del re they didn't account for what could happen if there was strong Surge and harmonic waves getting in Phase uh and the waves would suddenly be several feet higher than the design height of the pilings so suddenly all this stuff is churning about like a washing machine and it resulted in some great sections of docks floating around with their boats attached some boats floating around not attached to anything some docks floating around not attached to anything so this was the wakeup call they had seen the full scale model and suddenly they understood what the problem was frequently when you have prideful Engineers uh that didn't recognize the problem immediately and suddenly are confronted with it say oh well of course we'll do this the marina went through a couple of versions of that uh uh two sets of we'll call them the wave disruptors walls that protruded protruded from the entrance Channel about 2/3 across from One Direction and 2/3 across from the other direction with a gap for the boats to go through and the idea is that waves would come and the energy would be dissipated with these walls but they weren't up to the job a tremendous amount of energy can be generated and they soon discovered that wasn't enough and uh uh decided they would do a thorough engineering study and uh then they came up with the idea of well let's build a wall outside the entrance and maybe the waves will hit the wall and uh they tried that in the model and darned if it didn't work and so they started doing it um a full scale and here it is and it's worked pretty well Wetland Conservation is called the development of Marina del re the single most devastating blow to the protection of local wetlands in the late 50s to the early 60s 900 Acres of hollowed land was destroyed for its construction in its place in 1962 a new Harbor community was born today Marina del re Harbors thousands of boats and is a world-renowned port of call and now at over 50 years old is on the Press PRP of a complete overhaul and Redevelopment like the initial construction this undertaking is full of political wrangling and disagreement but once complete will continue to be one of the most prominent Harbors in the world

- Marina Del Rey Constuction History: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] for for the Marina del re site is a portion of the original Rancho laiona it is an unincorporated Island surrounded by the city of Los Angeles the elevation of most of the site was below maximum high tide and also subject to inundation from surrounding City areas the soil is largely silt and Clay the first step in construction was Excavating a continuous trench for the wall along with grading an inboard Dy to protect the work from the placement of the Bridge fill next Timber piles were driven every 4 ft the pile drivers worked from the access road that was between the trench and the Dyke two pile drivers were nated at times and periodically they had to work double shifts another crew followed up pouring reinforced concrete caps on the pile a layer of crush Rock was compacted at the bottom of the trench and then the wall footings were poured in alternate 50ft sections this type of pouring reduced the effect of shrinkage the contractor fabricated sturdy steel wall forms in 50 fft sections these could be moved and easily set up on the footing slabs the forms had smooth metal facing on the front side and inspection ports for checking the final placement of the rebar on the rear side after completion of the wall the armor Rock was placed down slope after placement of the armor Rock and the back filling of the wall the dredging contractor was allowed to Edge to the Finish improvements the Civic dredging the dredging contractor used the dredge which they brought down from Seattle it was powered by electricity and was on site for three years 14 million yards of material were dredged about half from the main Channel and the rest from the interior the Corp of Engineers was responsible for for the entrance Channel and the county was responsible for the rest combined contracts were used for better bids half of the hydraulic fill was used on site the good sand from the beach entrance was placed here on Doc wher Beach and the remaining 35% was sold by bids to adjacent property owners this is an area that is currently being filled the dredge pumps a slurry of mainly water and sand and fills an area and the water drains off leaving the soil behind [Applause] PCH uh Highway also known as Lincoln Boulevard this area used to be about 5 ft above the ground level of the adjacent Wetlands uh before the marina was built this area along the northeast boundary of the marina used to be a railroad rideway as you ride along the bike path you will note the long narrow buildings and the uh low ground level this area used to flood very frequently even in low-level storms with the completion of the dredging work began on the interior improvements it quickly became apparent that too much energy was entering the harbor the core of engineers immediately constructed a large scale model of the harbor at their experimental facility at Berg Mississippi which showed that an offshore Breakwater was by far the best protection since funding would take about two years funding and construction would take about two years the county immediately constructed steel sheet pile bulkheads at the channel curve restricting the opening to about 1/3 of the normal Channel width these barriers were lifted in place until the permanent Breakwater was completed thus allowing work to proceed on the Harbor infrastructure slips and Lease improvements this picture shows the completed Marina with the offshore Breakwater the runways of LAX may be seen just above and to the left during the construction of the Breakwater work proceeded on the roads water system sewers including a large sewage pumping station and a complete underground network of Power phone security and fire Communications Al also work on the lease's slips and buildings was able to proceed the Marina Del Rey was dedicated in April of 1965 for years the biona Creek Mosquito Abatement District did battle with the dreaded Sentinel Valley mosquitoes parking or being outdoors after dark tended to be hazardous with the completion of the Marina and The Filling of adjacent properties the problem disappeared the biona creek Mosquito Abatement District was dissolved

these models they figured out that if they built a breakwater these rocks would deflect waves and protect the marina and they built an actual one that still stands today protecting the marina from heavy weather events now that we've got the marina the next project is to combine Baldwin Hills and Kenneth on two bike paths and walkways that lead to the ocean that's where the planners and styles they want and what plans to use for landscaping if I were to build my own marina I'd want it to look like a tropical paradise like going to the South Pacific you're not alone on that one the planners created that a lot of the streets in Marina del Rey are named after islands and the palm trees are used to create that Island feel landscaping and building choices we make have big impacts on the animals that live here too Marina del Rey is extraordinary in that it is still affiliated with a natural marsh system its affiliated with dunes that have been restored there are still endangered species that nest and make their homes here and so while this is clearly an urban Harbor and it's dominated by the boats on the surface both above and below it steaming as a very vibrant ecosystem this part of California is actually in an area called a biodiversity hotspot recognized by Conservation International as one of a handful of really really important ecosystems that have lots of plants and animals that are only found in this area in fact by some estimates half of the plants in this part of Southern California are only found here and so their role is important not only to the local ecosystem but the global genetic reserve that we have in biodiversity I read that palm trees aren't native to this area doesn't it create problems if plants are brought in from outside ecosystems whatever plants you choose attract certain animals in the early days plants were chosen for that Island feel or simply because they were available so the eucalyptus trees that were planted grew tall and created homes for birds to nest in so what you're saying is that for every choice we make there's consequences the first shrubs that were planted here attracted feral cats rats and other pests and the wooden structures that were built brought termite so if we were going to build a marina the plans we would use and the resources we would need could affect the animals that come here there's more to it than that as the climate changes we have to select plants that will survive in the climate that's to come so as we plant trees we have to think decades into the future if the community gets involved they'll become more informed about the issues some people even volunteer to help gather information that will be used in the policy decisions a community scientist is just a normal stakeholder who gets involved in a local issue and helps collect data that informs that issue and that in turn informs the policy that is developed as a result there's been a lot of engagement meetings and events where community members are brought in to meet with the the individuals who are putting together these changes and voice their concerns and I've seen those concerns actually address and then put into the the plans that are being developed for the marina so if we know what the people love about the marina and we understand how it's being used we can make choices that will improve these characteristics so what are the people who use the marina say about it things that I like about Marina del Rey are it's a very very very small community of sailors and community members and lifeguards Coast Guard persons that work together to celebrate sailing and the sailing community so it's a wonderful place to be it's a whatever place to live it's a wonderful place important the beauty of sailing in Marina del Rey and also teaching sailing in Marina del Rey is that the winds are pretty consistent we can be in the harbor instructing and in our very safe little enclosed environment and then 15 minutes later we're out in the middle of the ocean now we're looking at Catalina we're looking at the mountains we see all of Southern California it's fabulous once you leave this Harbor you can go anywhere in the world this is my home this is where I leave from this is where I come back to you but I can go anywhere I want in the world we're in Marina del Rey right now it's about 550 in the morning we're getting ready to start our morning practice and we're about to go out on the water an amazing experience to be able to wake up and see the sunrise over the marina the Ducks of one of the most gorgeous experiences that anyone can ever have so coming here to row it's been like a dream come true yeah I agree Marie we get pretty lucky on how good the water conditions are here I'm from Washington where you know you have like two months on the water that are really nice to grow in the rest of your you and you get something what it's a nice area because it's very protected from the wind and other elements I love seeing like the sea lions and then every once in a while we get to see dolphins and they'll come up really close to our boats in it so they think it's a freshman here I thought they were sharks super and I got really scared but but it's really nice just getting to see the different wildlife that can live here you know it you know it's a man-made area but somehow they found their place here too the seal jumped on one of the boys boat they were as they were growing it into the dock he was just I think that was just like a funny expense local fishing club composed of people like myself that love to fish the fishing is actually really good it's a great place to launch your boat from because you're close to a lot of the really good fishing areas so you don't have too far to travel summertime is when you catch the exotics those are the fish that most of the fisherman really want like yellowtail yellowfin tuna Dorado they tend to travel with the warmer water so summer times better for them but when the water gets colder you can also catch lingcod rock cod those kind of fish they tend to go in deeper water but those are also you know highly sought after really good to eat so we have a project right here where we raise white sea bass it's funded by the Department of Fish and Wildlife and we get our hatchery fish from an operation in Carlsbad called hub SeaWorld Research Institute and we get little white sea bass they're about three inches long we raise them here in this pen all stocked by volunteer help it usually takes three to four months till they get to juvenile size like about 8 to 10 inches and at that point we release them and they're old enough to forage for themselves and hopefully avoid the Predators the seals in the birds and they grow to be 4050 pounds ten years ago you hardly saw any of them they were kind of rare and since cub SeaWorld started this program you see the sport both accounts go - ⚓ Annapolis Sailboat Show 2025 Full Tour, Boats, Gear & Sailing Inspiration! ⚓ Ep 343 Lady K Sailing: Kind: captions Language: en The Annapapolis Fall Sailboat Show is coming up quick. It's in October this year and it's going to be lit. This is the cruising sailboat paradise. Not to mention the new boats and vendors and sailing celebrities. Let me tell you why. [Music] [Applause] Anapapolis fall sailboat show is kind of the center of the sailing universe. Every October, sailors and vendors and manufacturers from all around the world rain down on Anapapolis like nowhere else in the world. And what's especially important is that it's more than just a sailboat show. It's cruisers mile marker zero. Let me explain. When people want to go sail in the Caribbean, they normally won't jump off of mainland US until November because it's hurricane season down in the islands. Insurance companies won't allow it. And for those who aren't insured, it's just bad business to go sail with the hurricanes. So, everybody gathers on the east coast of the US waiting for November. And the safest place and most convenient place to gather is Chesapeake Bay where Annapapolis is. Anapapolis is like any East Coast fishing village. Very historic and beautiful cobblestone with different color houses. But it also houses the Naval Academy. So, it's steeped in military tradition, museums, and things like that. It's also home to some of the best sailboat parts shopping to outfit your boat, some of the best sailboat racing, and home base for thousands of sailors. And every October, the town basically centers around the boat shows. First, it's the sailboats and then it's the less sophisticated boats later on. So, that fills the whole place up with cruisers. The anchorages go for miles up every river and cut all around Annapolis. And they'll all be absolutely full of cruising boats loaded down with jerry cans and Starlink dishes. It is a beautiful thing. And everyone goes to the show. The show is stuffed to the gills with vendors and boat makers showing off all their stuff. New boats get released here in Annapolis and the latest sailing gadgets are all getting shown off. You could spend a whole day just exploring the vendor tents, but one tent in particular interests a lot of people. The cruising content creators tent used to be called the YouTube tent. Anapapolis has long been the meeting place for people who make cruising content. We've got Distant Shores, Lauren Landers, MJ Sailing, The Rigging Doctor, Ryan and Sophie, Fair Isle, Gypsy, Parlay Revival, my personal favorite, SV Delos, Zingaro, Sam Holmes, and so many more. I don't know about you guys, but I am so stoked to meet all of these folks, especially Delos, because it's kind of them who sucked me into this whole sailing YouTube atmosphere. Anyway, and don't get me started on the sailboats. A full list is available on the Annapolis Sailboat Show website, as well as a map of the show with each dock, so you'll be able to see where every boat is parked, and you can kind of plan your route through the show. But the boats that caught my eye this year, the Allure 39 is one of them. This is an all aluminum cruiser with an integral centerboard, meaning the centerboard's part of the structure of the boat. Sale magazine said, "Aluminum boats that carry true integral centerboards and can take the ground with impunity have long been popular with French cruisers. However, until recently, they've gained very little traction in the United States. But this is starting to change as the French sort of mini conglomerate Grand Large Yaching, which owns two of these builders, Garcia and Allur, has pushed to gain them some exposure in the US. The debut of the 399 at the 2016 US Anapapolis Sailboat Show was part of that effort. Though it shares many attributes with a lot of other boats of this type, the 399 has some very cool features that you'll have to check out. I don't care what type of sailboats that you like. Aluminum boats are awesome and I can't wait to get aboard this one. Balance will also be there with some cuttingedge catamarans, the 502 and the 580. These guys are making a name for the way they make boats. It's like a supercar. The way they make a supercar is usually a carbon fiber tub that everything else sort of bolts onto. And Balance has started to do the same thing with cats. They make this carbon fiber framework and then build the rest of the boat around it. This is such cool technology and I can't wait to explore these balanced cats. I want to open every locker and see all the amazing carbon fiber work they did inside. Bento is of course one of the biggest exhibitors at this show with VIP tents and lots and lots of boats, but of interest this year are a couple of new and very important boats. Bento basically owns the cruising boat segment of the market with the most boats built and sold. So when they release a new 40 or 50footer, we need to pay attention. For 2025, they're bringing the all-new Oianis 47 and 52. These are peak cruising sailboats and what the market will be flooded with used versions of in about 15 years. So, we need to pay attention to these two boats. Catalina is bringing a bunch of the six series boats. These are the new ones and the one you should really look at is the 356. It's the smaller one of the lot, but being a mid-30footer, it's selling very, very well right now. And we should be seeing them in every marina in North America over the next 10 years. Get used to this boat because it is not going away. Next up, the Good Hope 65 by Edjoy Designs. These are expeditionary yachts meant to sail the whole world without limit. Lifting keels on boats built with redundancy and simplicity and ruggedness. This will be such a cool boat to get on and poke around because they're so rare to be seen sort of parked anywhere. More likely, you're going to see them on the news as the first boat to sail to some awful place no one wants to go, like the South Pole. A personal favorite of mine, the FAR40. You won't usually see these at the Anapapolis show. This is a raceboat that I adore and admire, and the folks over at the sailing school Oak Cliff Sailing have several of these FAR40s. They're bringing one to Annapapolis, presumably to promote their excellent sailing academy. So, it would be so cool to see a FAR40 sitting at a dock. Usually, I only get to see them with their rail in the water flying past me at Mach 3. Halberg Razi is always at the show and this year is no exception. If you want to see how the other half live, they'll have the 340 and her big sister, the 44 on for the show. These are the cream of the crop cruising boats built to spare no expense, and getting on one is always a treat. And speaking of treats, Moody will have the DS48 on display. Get on this thing and count how many times your mouse says, "Wow." They're designing this boat to look and feel more like a high-end power yacht, a baby super yacht. Because they say too many rich people are buying motor yachts, so they should buy this instead because it feels and looks and acts like a rich motor yacht, but it's still a sailboat. And I'm inclined to agree. These are magnificent and getting more people sailing, even rich people, is a good thing. Oh, and speaking of super yachts, Nautter Swan will be swinging for the fences this year. They're bringing the Swan 78. They say all of Naut's knowhow and experience in racing and cruising yachts has been incorporated into the new Bluewater Swan 78, a direct descendant of their original Swan 80 and 82 models from the past. This boat's slightly shorter overall in order to meet the EC 24 meter length limitation, but it's beamier and more powerful. The modern hull lines, including a wide stern above the waterline, twin rudders, and a racing oriented keel, are a reflection of the rapid evolution of yacht design over the past years. Propelled by experiences made on their round the world races and other offshore events, the new boat is faster, safer, easier to control, and as a result, more comfortable at sea. This boat will be a showstopper. So, if you want to get on it, go to it early. And speaking of early, some Anapapolis Sailboat Show advice for you. I go every year, so hopefully some of my mistakes will help you. Go early. I go to the website and check out all the boats, and I make a short list of the boats I really want to see. And then when I go to the show, I go on the first public day, usually the Thursday, and show up before the gates open. And when I get in, I beline to the biggest boat on my list. The bigger boats mean busier. Then I see the second biggest boat, and so on. Once I've seen all my target boats, I head for lunch and then try to crawl through the vendor tents the rest of the day. On day two, usually Friday or Saturday, it's more about wandering aimlessly and seeing the sites and whatever boats I missed. I love to get on the water taxi boats, too. Slip the captain a couple bucks and kick back while he drives people around all day up all the cuts and rivers around Annapolis. By the water is the best way to see all the cruising boats that are mored and anchored out. These folks are headed south and the boats they live on are out in the harbor. They are the real sailboats of this show. I love seeing the arches and Davidits and enclosures and other cruising modifications. It's so much fun. I'll be headed to the show, too. I've never been in the content creator section before. I always prefer to wander around the show, but this year I sent Annapapolis an an email and I asked them if I can be in the YouTube section. I suspect they might say no because I'm just a sailing dude on YouTube and I'm not a live cruiser, but we'll see. Either way, I will be at the show for several days this year and I hope I can see you guys there. Stop me so that we can have a chat. Just don't buy me a drink. I've been alcohol-free for 2 years, 2 months, and 20 days. I'm also thinking about taking my Hobie Cat down there so I can sail around all the rivers in Annapolis. I think that would be a super cool way to see sort of all the cruising boats that are anchored out ripping around on a Hobie Cat. So, if you want to get down on that, let me know. A huge shout out here to the people who make these videos possible. Patrons who give as little as a couple of bucks an episode. I truly couldn't make these without you. If you want to help me get more people sailing, get more people into this beautiful sport of ours, please consider becoming a patron. Until next week, friends, keep the heavy side down, but not too far down. We'll see you.
